sections.title: Overview
sections.html:
Skateboarding

My most consistently-alive creative spark — built into a deliberate progression system instead of leaving progress to chance.

This domain is a coach's playbook for my own skating. It maps what unlocks what, captures the training wisdom with the most leverage on long-term progress, records honestly where I'm at, curates the sources I mine for insight, and turns all of it into sessions that actually move the needle.

How the pieces fit

SOURCES ──digest──▶ JOURNAL ──extract──▶ WISDOM
                                            │
TRICKS (the map) ◀──updates── ASSESSMENT   │
   │                   ▲                    │
   └───────────────────┴──── feed ──────────┘
                       ▼
                   SESSIONS  ← what I actually do
                       ▲
                    SPOTS  ← where (matches the goal to the place)
  1. TRICKS.md — the map. Tricks, movements, drills, and skills as a graph with explicit Prerequisites and Unlocks edges. The source of truth for "what should I work on next."
  2. WISDOM.md — the distilled high-leverage principles. What training actually compounds, ranked by leverage. This is the brain of the system.
  3. SOURCES.md — curated YouTube channels + podcasts I mine, each with a "what to mine it for" note. I maintain this list.
    • MINING.md — how content becomes wisdom: the hybrid mining method (Lane 1 auto-transcript, Lane 2 voice-memo for visual technique, Lane 3 hybrid).
  4. ASSESSMENT.md — living record of my current skill set (have / learning / want), wired to the trick map, with dated snapshots. Companion: TRICKMATRIX.md — the full 199-trick vocabulary with landed/target rep counts, my live rep-tracker (ASSESSMENT stays the honest status). Imported from Trello, now maintained here.
  5. SESSIONS.md — the repeatable method for planning an effective session, plus ready-to-run templates.
  6. SPOTS.md — where I skate, each spot tagged with what it's good for, so session planning matches the day's goal to the right place (e.g. low transition → stall reps). Obstacle photo galleries (from Trello): SPOTS-ORLA.md · SPOTS-IAPI.md.
  7. JOURNAL/ — dated entries digesting content: source → wisdom extracted → updates pushed to WISDOM / TRICKS / ASSESSMENT.

The loop (how it grows, little by little)

  • Consume a video/podcast from SOURCES → write a JOURNAL entry digesting it.
  • The journal entry extracts wisdom (→ WISDOM) and may add nodes/edges (→ TRICKS).
  • Periodically reassess (→ ASSESSMENT) so current state stays honest.
  • Plan a session (→ SESSIONS) from assessment + wisdom + the map.
  • Skate, film, review → the session feeds the next assessment.

Start here

  • Cold? Read WISDOM.md (5 min) — it changes how you plan everything else.
  • Planning today's skate? Go straight to SESSIONS.md → "Session template."
  • Watched something good? JOURNAL/ → copy the entry template.

sections.title: Trick Map
sections.html:
Tricks, Drills & Skills — The Map

How to read this map

This is a graph, not a checklist. Every node is one of four types, and every node names what comes before and after it so you can plan a real practice path.

Node types:

  • [TRICK] — a named maneuver you land (ollie, kickflip, 50-50).
  • [MOVEMENT] — a body/board mechanic that recurs across many tricks (pop, scoop, catch, shoulder alignment). Learn it once, reuse it everywhere.
  • [DRILL] — a deliberate practice exercise, usually stationary or low-stakes, that isolates one mechanic (ollies over a stick, manual rolls, primo flicks).
  • [SKILL] — a broad capacity that grows across your whole skating (board control, fear management, terrain reading, switch competency).

Edge convention — these labels are greppable, every trick node has both:

  • Prerequisites: what you must reliably own before this is worth grinding. If you can't do these, you're wasting reps.
  • Unlocks: what this node makes accessible next. Follow these forward to plan progression.
  • Discipline (on [TRICK] nodes only): which of the five lanes it trains — flatground · transition · ledge · rail · gap (use a comma for tricks that span two). Greppable like the edges; PlanSession.ts reads it to balance frontiers across disciplines (WISDOM #14).

How to use it: find where you are, look at Unlocks, pick the next node whose Prerequisites you already own. When a trick fights you, drop to its underlying [MOVEMENT] nodes and the [DRILL] that isolates them. (This diagnose-down-then-climb-up move is principle #2 in WISDOM.md.)

Progression Chains (the spine)

Five-plus arrow sequences. Each is a lane; the Movements section shows where lanes share mechanics.

  • (a) Foundation / balance: pushing → rolling comfort → tic-tac → kickturn (frontside + backside) → pumping → carving
  • (b) The ollie line: stationary ollie → rolling ollie → ollie up a curb → ollie over an obstacle → ollie down stairs/gap → ollie onto/off objects
  • (c) Flip-trick line: ollie → shuvit → pop shuvit → kickflip → heelflip → varial kickflip → varial heelflip → 360 flip (tre)
  • (d) Rotational / shuvit line: shuvit → pop shuvit → frontside pop shuvit → 360 shuvit → bigspin → 360 flip
  • (e) Manual / balance line: rolling balance → manual → nose manual → manual the length of a box → manual → kickflip out / into manuals
  • (f) Transition / ramp line: rolling on a ramp → pumping → kickturn on transition → drop-in → fakie rock → rock to fakie → axle stall → 50-50 stall → backside grind
  • (g) Stance expansion: fakie rolling → fakie ollie → nollie → switch pushing → switch ollie → switch flips and pop shuvit → bigspin → half-cab → full cab

Foundations

[SKILL] Board Control

The umbrella capacity all of this feeds. Grows forever.

  • Prerequisites: none — you start building it on day one.
  • Unlocks: every trick node below. Low board control caps how fast everything else comes.

[MOVEMENT] Pushing & Rolling

  • Prerequisites: standing on the board, comfort with the board moving.
  • Unlocks: rolling comfort, tic-tac, every rolling trick.

[DRILL] Roll-and-Balance

Roll across flat, feet in trick position, find the bolts by feel without looking down.

  • Prerequisites: Pushing & Rolling.
  • Unlocks: manual foot awareness, rolling ollie comfort.

[TRICK] Tic-Tac

Lifting the nose with weight shifts to turn and self-propel on flat.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: rolling comfort, [MOVEMENT] Weight Shift.
  • Unlocks: kickturn, nose-lift feel that feeds the ollie's front foot.

[TRICK] Kickturn (Frontside + Backside)

Pivot on the back wheels to change direction.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Tic-Tac, Weight Shift, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Alignment.
  • Unlocks: pumping, carving, kickturn on transition (transition line).

[SKILL] Pumping & Carving

Generating speed without pushing, through rhythmic weight and turn.

  • Prerequisites: Kickturn, rolling comfort.
  • Unlocks: transition line (ramp speed without pushing), bowl/flow skating.

The Ollie Line

[MOVEMENT] Pop

The snap of the tail to the ground — the engine of nearly every airborne trick.

  • Prerequisites: Roll-and-Balance, tail awareness.
  • Unlocks: every ollie and flip trick. Shared by kickflip, heelflip, pop shuvit, all transition airs.
  • Coaching cues (mined from Norman Woods' Rookie Mistakes — this is where rookies bleed the most progress):
    • Squat deep — get the knee to 90° or below; deeper = more pop and more landing control. Most beginners barely bend (120°) and wonder why there's no height.
    • Back heel UP — toes/ball of the back foot only, never heel-down. Heel-down is a piston (one push); heel-up makes the ankle a lever (push down and lift).
    • Toes on the center of the tail — not hanging over the edge. Overhang turns the foot into a piston and caps everything downstream (no clean pop-shuvit, kickflip, or fs 180).
    • Pop the instant you've squatted — nervous-system lag means you actually fire as you start to rise (the sweet spot). Intending to pop late = legs already straight = no height.
    • Only as high as the obstacle needs — over-popping is a fear response; it makes you slip out of grinds or land with uncontrollable force.
    • Arms drive it — drop them as you crouch, throw them UP first as you explode (sprint mechanics — arms and legs in tandem).
    • Don't push the board down early — after the pop, suck knees up, float, let gravity bring it down, then crouch to absorb. Pushing down caps height and dumps impact into your joints.

[MOVEMENT] Front-Foot Slide / Drag

Dragging the front foot up the grip to level and lift the board after the pop.

  • Prerequisites: Pop.
  • Unlocks: ollie height, the leveling motion every flip trick reuses.

[MOVEMENT] Level Shoulders

Keeping shoulders square and level over the board so the board stays level in the air.

  • Prerequisites: none — train it from the first ollie.
  • Unlocks: clean ollies, all flip tricks (prevents the board going crooked).
  • Coaching cues (Rookie Mistakes): keep the back under 45° — hinge from the knees, not the waist (the hidden fault: people "bend their knees" by folding the back forward and don't realize it). Look at your feet/board to build the neural map and catch bad foot placement — even advanced skaters watch the board 70–80% of the time, glancing at the obstacle only to set the angle.

[MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation (Wind-and-Unwind)

The continuous shoulder wind/unwind that drives every rotational trick. Wind the shoulders opposite the turn, then unwind in one continuous motion — pop at the exact moment the shoulders are parallel over the board and you're at full squat. The killer error: winding, then stopping rotation to pop, then resuming — that kills the trick.

  • Prerequisites: Pop, Level Shoulders.
  • Unlocks: fs/bs 180, 360s, bigspin, half/full cab — every rotational trick. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #02. Don't copy pros' "late" rotation look as a beginner — that's years of muscle memory.)

[DRILL] Stationary Ollies Over a Stick

Pop ollies standing still, clearing a low stick/rail to force height and front-foot drag.

  • Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide.
  • Unlocks: rolling ollie, obstacle clearance confidence.

[TRICK] Stationary Ollie

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide, Level Shoulders.
  • Unlocks: Rolling Ollie.

[TRICK] Rolling Ollie

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Stationary Ollie, rolling comfort, [SKILL] Commitment.
  • Unlocks: Ollie up a Curb, all flip tricks (a flip trick is a manipulated rolling ollie).

[TRICK] Ollie up a Curb

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie.
  • Unlocks: Ollie over an Obstacle, ledge approaches (grinds line).

[TRICK] Ollie over an Obstacle

  • Discipline: flatground, gap
  • Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Stationary Ollies Over a Stick (drill transfer).
  • Unlocks: Ollie down Stairs/Gap, [SKILL] Terrain Reading at speed.

[TRICK] Ollie down Stairs / Gap

  • Discipline: gap
  • Prerequisites: Ollie over an Obstacle, [SKILL] Commitment (fear management).
  • Unlocks: ollie onto/off objects, flip tricks down gaps, the whole "send it" tier.

Flip Tricks

All flip tricks are a [TRICK] Rolling Ollie plus a flick. They all share Pop, Level Shoulders, and a Catch.

[MOVEMENT] Catch

Stopping the board's rotation/flip with the feet and riding away — the difference between a "land" and a "credit."

  • Prerequisites: Pop, board awareness in the air.
  • Unlocks: clean kickflips, heelflips, every flip trick's makeable version.
  • Coaching cues (Rookie Mistakes): land on toes + ball of foot, then squat to absorb — ankle → knee → hips work as shocks. Landing on heels removes the ankle as a shock point and your joints eat the impact (heel bruises, "hot pockets"). Suck knees to chest for height and clearance — needs hip-flexor mobility (tight flexors are why beginners can't tuck). (Mined: #20, #13.)

[MOVEMENT] Flick (Kick Flick)

The toe/ankle snap off the corner of the nose that spins the board on its long axis.

  • Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide.
  • Unlocks: kickflip, varial kickflip, 360 flip.

[MOVEMENT] Heel Flick

The heel snap off the toe-side edge that flips the board the opposite way.

  • Prerequisites: Pop, Level Shoulders.
  • Unlocks: heelflip, varial heelflip.

[DRILL] Primo Flicks / Flip-and-Catch

Stationary: flip the board with the flick and catch it with feet, no air, to learn rotation speed and catch timing.

  • Prerequisites: Flick or Heel Flick.
  • Unlocks: Kickflip, Heelflip (builds the catch without committing to the pop).

[TRICK] Kickflip

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Flick, Catch, Level Shoulders; Primo Flicks drill.
  • Shared movements: Pop + Level Shoulders + Catch (same as Heelflip).
  • Unlocks: Varial Kickflip, 360 Flip, kickflip-into-manual, kickflip down stairs.

[TRICK] Heelflip

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Heel Flick, Catch, Level Shoulders.
  • Shared movements: Pop + Level Shoulders + Catch (same as Kickflip — that's why landing one accelerates the other).
  • Unlocks: Varial Heelflip, heelflip variations.

[TRICK] Frontside Flip

A frontside 180 and a kickflip at the same time — the board flips while you and it rotate 180° frontside.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Kickflip, fs 180 (ollie), [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Catch.
  • Shared movements: Flick + Shoulder Rotation (wind-and-unwind) + Catch.
  • Unlocks: fs flip variations, the frontside flip-trick family. (Blind catch — you rotate away from the board, so committing the shoulders + spotting the catch late is the crux.)

[TRICK] Backside Flip

A backside 180 and a kickflip at once — the board flips while you and it rotate 180° backside. Mirror of the frontside flip, but the catch is visible (you rotate toward the board), so spotting the landing is more forgiving than the fs flip's blind catch.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Kickflip, bs 180 (ollie), [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Catch.
  • Shared movements: Flick + Shoulder Rotation (wind-and-unwind) + Catch.
  • Unlocks: bs flip variations, the backside flip-trick family.
  • Where Tiago is: 2/10 — early, landing the occasional one. Long road but it's started.

[TRICK] Varial Kickflip

Kickflip + backside pop shuvit rotation at once.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Kickflip, Pop Shuvit.
  • Unlocks: Varial Heelflip, hardflip, 360 flip approaches.

[TRICK] 360 Flip (Tre Flip)

Kickflip + 360 shuvit scoop together — a peak intermediate trick.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Kickflip, 360 Shuvit, strong Scoop.
  • Unlocks: advanced flip combos, the "you've arrived" intermediate milestone.

Rotational / Shuvit Tricks

[MOVEMENT] Scoop

The back-foot scrape/whip that spins the board flat (horizontal axis). Power source for all shuvit-family tricks.

  • Prerequisites: Roll-and-Balance, back-foot control.
  • Unlocks: shuvit, pop shuvit, 360 shuvit, bigspin, and the rotation half of varial kickflip / 360 flip.

[TRICK] Shuvit (Pop-less / Ground Shuvit)

Board spins 180° flat under you, no pop.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Scoop, Roll-and-Balance.
  • Unlocks: Pop Shuvit.

[TRICK] Pop Shuvit

Shuvit with an ollie pop so the board spins in the air.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Shuvit, Pop, Catch.
  • Shared movements: Pop + Scoop.
  • Unlocks: Frontside Pop Shuvit, 360 Shuvit, Varial Kickflip, Bigspin.

[TRICK] Frontside Pop Shuvit

Pop shuvit spun the harder (frontside) direction.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Pop Shuvit.
  • Unlocks: frontside flip variations, FS bigspin.

[TRICK] 360 Shuvit

Full 360° board rotation, no flip.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Pop Shuvit, strong Scoop.
  • Unlocks: Bigspin, 360 Flip (the rotation half of the tre).

[TRICK] Bigspin

360 shuvit + 180 body rotation together.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: 360 Shuvit, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Alignment (body wind-up).
  • Unlocks: bigspin flip, half-cab/cab body-rotation feel (stance line).

Manuals & Balance

[MOVEMENT] Weight Shift

Controlled fore/aft shift over the wheels — the core of balance tricks and tic-tac.

  • Prerequisites: rolling comfort.
  • Unlocks: Tic-Tac, Manual, Nose Manual.

[DRILL] Manual Rolls

Roll into a wheelie, hold the balance point a few feet, set down clean. Repeat for distance.

  • Prerequisites: Weight Shift, Roll-and-Balance.
  • Unlocks: Manual, Nose Manual.

[TRICK] Manual

Rolling wheelie on the back wheels, held for distance.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Weight Shift, Manual Rolls drill.
  • Unlocks: Nose Manual, Manual the length of a box, ollie-into-manual, flip-into/out-of-manual combos.

[TRICK] Nose Manual

Wheelie on the front wheels — harder balance point.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Manual.
  • Unlocks: nose-manual combos, ledge manual lines.

[TRICK] Manual the Length of a Box

Holding a manual the full length of a manny pad.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Manual, [SKILL] Commitment.
  • Unlocks: in/out combos (ollie → manual → kickflip out), the manual-tricks lane in lines.

Grinds & Slides (intro)

[SKILL] Terrain Reading

Judging ledge height, speed needed, run-up, and wax before you commit.

  • Prerequisites: Ollie over an Obstacle, real session mileage.
  • Unlocks: safe grind/slide approaches, gap selection, every spot decision.

[MOVEMENT] Ollie-to-Lock

Ollieing onto an edge and landing locked into a grind/slide position.

  • Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Level Shoulders, Terrain Reading.
  • Unlocks: 50-50, boardslide, all ledge tricks.

[TRICK] 50-50 Grind

Both trucks grinding a ledge/rail.

  • Discipline: ledge, rail
  • Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Ollie-to-Lock, Terrain Reading.
  • Unlocks: 5-0 grind, nosegrind, crooked grind.

[TRICK] Boardslide

Sliding on the board's underside across an obstacle.

  • Discipline: ledge, rail
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Ollie-to-Lock, [SKILL] Commitment.
  • Unlocks: lipslide, tailslide, the slide family.

[TRICK] Noseslide

Sliding on the underside of the nose across a ledge, board perpendicular.

  • Discipline: ledge
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Ollie-to-Lock, nose control, [SKILL] Terrain Reading.
  • Unlocks: Switch Noseslide, tailslide, noseslide variations (nollie-in, shuv-out). (Tiago owns this BS regular.)

[TRICK] Switch Noseslide

Noseslide in switch stance.

  • Discipline: ledge
  • Prerequisites: Noseslide (regular), Switch Ollie, [SKILL] Switch Competency.
  • Unlocks: switch ledge lines, the switch slide family. (Target — never landed yet.)

[TRICK] Switch 50-50

50-50 grind in switch stance.

  • Discipline: ledge, rail
  • Prerequisites: 50-50 Grind (regular), Switch Ollie, [SKILL] Switch Competency.
  • Unlocks: switch grind family, switch ledge lines. (Target — never landed yet; owns FS 50-50, switch is the stance gap.)

[TRICK] Crooked Grind (FS)

A nosegrind cocked across the ledge — grinding on the front truck with the nose angled into the ledge.

  • Discipline: ledge
  • Prerequisites: 50-50 Grind, nose-lock / nosegrind feel, Ollie-to-Lock, [SKILL] Terrain Reading.
  • Unlocks: crooked variations, the nose-grind ledge family. (Front-truck-only balance is the crux — the nose wants to slip; committing forward into the ledge holds the lock. Target — never landed yet.)

Transition Basics

[TRICK] Drop-in

Committing from the coping into the ramp.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: rolling comfort, [SKILL] Commitment (this is 90% fear).
  • Unlocks: all ramp/bowl skating, pumping for speed, stalls.

[TRICK] Kickturn on Transition

Pivoting on the transition wall to come back down.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Kickturn (flat), Drop-in, Pumping.
  • Unlocks: carving lines, higher kickturns, stall setups.

[TRICK] Fakie Rock / Rock to Fakie

Rolling up, stalling the front trucks over the coping, coming back fakie.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Drop-in, Pumping, [SKILL] Fakie comfort.
  • Unlocks: rock 'n' roll, axle stalls, coping tricks.

[TRICK] Axle Stall (50-50 Stall)

Stalling both trucks on the coping, then re-entering.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Kickturn on Transition, Fakie Rock.
  • Unlocks: Backside Grind (transition), disasters, the coping-trick family.

[TRICK] Backside Disaster

Ollie/tap the tail over the coping so the board lands rocked on the lip (front truck over, deck on the coping), then roll back in fakie.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Axle Stall, Rock to Fakie, [SKILL] Commitment.
  • Unlocks: disaster variations, the coping-trick family. (The crux is committing the tap over the lip and trusting the rock — half-rolled-away means the commitment isn't all the way there yet.)

[TRICK] Backside / Frontside Grind (Transition)

Grinding the coping along the transition (not just stalling on it). Backside (back to the ramp) is the standard first grind; frontside (facing out) is harder — the lock and the re-entry are both blinder.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Axle Stall, Kickturn on Transition, Pumping (for grind speed).
  • Unlocks: longer grinds, grind-to-stall combos, the coping-trick family.
  • Where Tiago is: BS — stalls 6/10, grind-through not yet. FS — 2/10, just starting to lock.

[TRICK] Frontside Tailstall (Transition)

Ride up the transition, stall the tail on the coping (board pointing up/out), then drop back in.

  • Discipline: transition
  • Prerequisites: Drop-in, Kickturn on Transition, Pumping, [SKILL] Fakie Comfort (for the re-entry).
  • Unlocks: tailblock stalls, tailstall-to-fakie, the lip-trick family.
  • Practice note: grind reps on a low wall — IAPI's smaller mini ramp / small quarter (close-in, convenient) or ORLA's lowest transitions (every height available). Low consequence + high rep count is exactly how a stall trick gets owned. See SPOTS.md. (Target — new goal.)

Stance Expansion (fakie / switch / nollie)

[SKILL] Switch Competency

Skating your unnatural stance as if it were normal — doubles your whole trick list.

  • Prerequisites: solid regular-stance ollie + a flip trick.
  • Unlocks: every switch trick; full-cab; the "second skater inside you."

[TRICK] Fakie Ollie

Ollie while rolling backwards.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, [SKILL] Fakie comfort.
  • Unlocks: Half-Cab, fakie flip tricks.

[TRICK] Nollie

Ollie off the nose while rolling forward.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Level Shoulders, nose-pop feel (from Nose Manual / Tic-Tac).
  • Unlocks: Nollie Frontside 180, nollie flip tricks, nollie line.

[TRICK] Nollie Frontside 180

Nollie with a 180° frontside body+board rotation.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Nollie, fs 180 feel, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Level Shoulders.
  • Unlocks: nollie fs flip, the nollie rotation line.
  • Where Tiago is: 7/10 but feels weird every rep — high make-rate masking a mechanic fault (likely an early shoulder push, or popping off the toe instead of clean off the nose). Film it: the fix is probably one cue, and it's what's capping it below ✅.

[TRICK] Switch Ollie

Rolling ollie in switch stance.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Switch Competency (developing).
  • Unlocks: switch flip tricks, switch manuals, doubling the flip-trick lane.

[TRICK] Half-Cab

Fakie 180 ollie (half a caballerial).

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Fakie Ollie, Shoulder Alignment.
  • Unlocks: Full Cab, half-cab flip.

[TRICK] Full Cab (Caballerial)

Fakie 360 ollie.

  • Discipline: flatground
  • Prerequisites: Half-Cab, Bigspin body-rotation feel.
  • Unlocks: cab flips, advanced fakie line.

Recurring Movements (reference)

These are the shared mechanics. Master a movement once and every trick that lists it gets cheaper.

[MOVEMENT] What it is Tricks that reuse it
Pop Snap of the tail to the ground Every ollie, kickflip, heelflip, pop shuvit, all airs
Front-Foot Slide Dragging the front foot to level/lift Ollie height, kickflip flick, all flips
Level Shoulders Square, level shoulders over the board Clean ollie, kickflip, heelflip, nollie, ollie-to-lock
Catch Stopping rotation/flip with the feet Every flip trick, pop shuvit, bigspin
Flick Toe snap off the nose corner Kickflip, varial kickflip, 360 flip
Heel Flick Heel snap off the toe edge Heelflip, varial heelflip
Scoop Back-foot whip that spins the board flat Shuvit, pop shuvit, 360 shuvit, bigspin, varial kickflip, 360 flip
Weight Shift Controlled fore/aft balance shift Tic-tac, manual, nose manual
Shoulder Alignment Winding/aligning shoulders for rotation Kickturn, bigspin, half-cab, full cab
Ollie-to-Lock Ollieing into a locked grind/slide position 50-50, boardslide, all ledge tricks

Key transfer insight: kickflip and heelflip share Pop + Level Shoulders + Catch — landing one makes the other dramatically faster. varial kickflip and 360 flip are just kickflip's Flick combined with the Scoop from the shuvit lane — which is why you build the shuvit line in parallel with the flip line, not after it.

Core Skills (reference)

Broad capacities that grow across everything. They gate progress more than any single trick.

[SKILL] What it is Fed by / Gates
Board Control Overall command of the board Fed by every drill; gates the ceiling of all tricks
Commitment / Fear Management Spot-checking, then committing fully Gates drop-in, stairs/gaps, boardslides, rails — the #1 limiter on intermediate progress
Terrain Reading Judging spots, speed, run-up, obstacles Gates grinds, slides, gaps, transition lines
Switch Competency Skating unnatural stance fluently Doubles the entire trick list; gates switch/nollie lanes
Fakie Comfort Rolling and popping backwards Gates fakie ollie, half-cab, rock to fakie, transition
Pumping & Carving Speed generation without pushing Gates all transition/bowl skating
Centering Finding the spin/balance axis under your feet The master balance fundamental — gates spins (360 shuvit, tre, bigspin), manuals, and clean catches. (Mullen: "360s taught me more about the essence of balancing than any other move… being centered helps with every aspect of a trick.")
Distance / Landing-Zone Calibration Judging how much space the board needs to clear and land on an obstacle A separate perceptual skill from the trick itself — gates gaps, stairs, ledges, manuals onto objects. Trainable on its own (roll-up-and-stop on a mark, measured gaps), not just by attempting the trick. (Mullen ran into obstacles for a year learning street despite owning every flip — "I looked like I had no depth perception.")

Practice principle: when a [TRICK] stalls, you almost never need more reps of the trick — you need a [DRILL] that isolates the weak [MOVEMENT], or a [SKILL] (usually Commitment, sometimes Centering or Distance Calibration) that you haven't grown yet. Diagnose down the graph, then climb back up. And when nothing moves after honest reps, it's a missing principle, not a rep deficit (WISDOM #15) — switch to a diagnosis session.

Trick Invention & Design

How new tricks are actually made — distilled from Rodney Mullen, who invented most of the modern flatground vocabulary (WISDOM #16). Useful not just for inventing, but for learning anything new: it tells you where a trick comes from, so you know what to own first.

1. Port an owned move to a new plane or stance, then diagnose the one missing fix. A "new" trick is rarely learned whole. The flatground ollie was vert's tail-scoop taken from a vertical to a horizontal plane — and the only genuinely new piece was dragging the front foot up the board to level the "seesaw." Find the parent move you already own; isolate the single mechanic that's different; drill that.

2. Reverse-engineer from your own bails. The kickflip came from Mullen watching how the board flipped when his front foot slipped off during a failed ollie — then deliberately timing that flip after the pop. Your bails are showing you the mechanic of your next trick. Film them; don't just dismiss them.

3. One mechanical reframe often unlocks a stuck trick. The impossible only worked once he treated his back foot as a fixed axle for the board to wrap around — not more force, a different model of what the foot is doing. When a trick fights you, try re-describing what one body part is for, not trying harder.

4. Chase generative primitives, not dead-end novelties. Some tricks are platforms that open a whole category (ollie, kickflip — "a key that opened the door to an unlimited number of new tricks"); others are awkward one-offs (Mullen on the impossible: "a more awkward trick" with "limitations"). Before grinding a trick, ask what does owning this unlock? Spend reps where the Unlocks edge is richest. This is the map-level version of building the shuvit and flip lines in parallel (WISDOM #8).

5. Constraints are design prompts. Tony Hawk invented ollie-into-airs-and-grab-late precisely because he lacked the mass to blast airs the normal way. A physical limitation is a reason to find a different technique, not evidence you can't do the trick.

6. Verbalize the mechanics. Mullen's coaches made him break each new trick into repeatable steps out loud until the picture was clear. Saying the sub-movements exposes the gap — it's a learning tool on top of filming.
sections.text: Tricks, Drills Skills — The Map How to read this map This is a graph , not a checklist. Every node is one of four types, and every node names what comes before and after it so you can plan a real practice path. Node types: [TRICK] — a named maneuver you land (ollie, kickflip, 50-50). [MOVEMENT] — a body/board mechanic that recurs across many tricks (pop, scoop, catch, shoulder alignment). Learn it once, reuse it everywhere. [DRILL] — a deliberate practice exercise, usually stationary or low-stakes, that isolates one mechanic (ollies over a stick, manual rolls, primo flicks). [SKILL] — a broad capacity that grows across your whole skating (board control, fear management, terrain reading, switch competency). Edge convention — these labels are greppable, every trick node has both: Prerequisites: what you must reliably own before this is worth grinding. If you can't do these, you're wasting reps. Unlocks: what this node makes accessible next. Follow these forward to plan progression. Discipline (on [TRICK] nodes only): which of the five lanes it trains — flatground · transition · ledge · rail · gap (use a comma for tricks that span two). Greppable like the edges; PlanSession.ts reads it to balance frontiers across disciplines ( WISDOM #14 ). How to use it: find where you are, look at Unlocks , pick the next node whose Prerequisites you already own. When a trick fights you, drop to its underlying [MOVEMENT] nodes and the [DRILL] that isolates them. (This diagnose-down-then-climb-up move is principle #2 in WISDOM.md .) Progression Chains (the spine) Five-plus arrow sequences. Each is a lane; the Movements section shows where lanes share mechanics. (a) Foundation / balance: pushing → rolling comfort → tic-tac → kickturn (frontside + backside) → pumping → carving (b) The ollie line: stationary ollie → rolling ollie → ollie up a curb → ollie over an obstacle → ollie down stairs/gap → ollie onto/off objects (c) Flip-trick line: ollie → shuvit → pop shuvit → kickflip → heelflip → varial kickflip → varial heelflip → 360 flip (tre) (d) Rotational / shuvit line: shuvit → pop shuvit → frontside pop shuvit → 360 shuvit → bigspin → 360 flip (e) Manual / balance line: rolling balance → manual → nose manual → manual the length of a box → manual → kickflip out / into manuals (f) Transition / ramp line: rolling on a ramp → pumping → kickturn on transition → drop-in → fakie rock → rock to fakie → axle stall → 50-50 stall → backside grind (g) Stance expansion: fakie rolling → fakie ollie → nollie → switch pushing → switch ollie → switch flips and pop shuvit → bigspin → half-cab → full cab Foundations [SKILL] Board Control The umbrella capacity all of this feeds. Grows forever. Prerequisites: none — you start building it on day one. Unlocks: every trick node below. Low board control caps how fast everything else comes. [MOVEMENT] Pushing Rolling Prerequisites: standing on the board, comfort with the board moving. Unlocks: rolling comfort, tic-tac , every rolling trick. [DRILL] Roll-and-Balance Roll across flat, feet in trick position, find the bolts by feel without looking down. Prerequisites: Pushing Rolling. Unlocks: manual foot awareness, rolling ollie comfort. [TRICK] Tic-Tac Lifting the nose with weight shifts to turn and self-propel on flat. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: rolling comfort, [MOVEMENT] Weight Shift. Unlocks: kickturn , nose-lift feel that feeds the ollie's front foot. [TRICK] Kickturn (Frontside + Backside) Pivot on the back wheels to change direction. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Tic-Tac, Weight Shift, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Alignment. Unlocks: pumping , carving , kickturn on transition (transition line). [SKILL] Pumping Carving Generating speed without pushing, through rhythmic weight and turn. Prerequisites: Kickturn, rolling comfort. Unlocks: transition line (ramp speed without pushing), bowl/flow skating. The Ollie Line [MOVEMENT] Pop The snap of the tail to the ground — the engine of nearly every airborne trick. Prerequisites: Roll-and-Balance, tail awareness. Unlocks: every ollie and flip trick. Shared by kickflip , heelflip , pop shuvit , all transition airs. Coaching cues (mined from Norman Woods' Rookie Mistakes — this is where rookies bleed the most progress): Squat deep — get the knee to 90° or below; deeper = more pop and more landing control. Most beginners barely bend ( 120°) and wonder why there's no height. Back heel UP — toes/ball of the back foot only, never heel-down. Heel-down is a piston (one push); heel-up makes the ankle a lever (push down and lift). Toes on the center of the tail — not hanging over the edge. Overhang turns the foot into a piston and caps everything downstream (no clean pop-shuvit, kickflip, or fs 180). Pop the instant you've squatted — nervous-system lag means you actually fire as you start to rise (the sweet spot). Intending to pop late = legs already straight = no height. Only as high as the obstacle needs — over-popping is a fear response; it makes you slip out of grinds or land with uncontrollable force. Arms drive it — drop them as you crouch, throw them UP first as you explode (sprint mechanics — arms and legs in tandem). Don't push the board down early — after the pop, suck knees up, float , let gravity bring it down, then crouch to absorb. Pushing down caps height and dumps impact into your joints. [MOVEMENT] Front-Foot Slide / Drag Dragging the front foot up the grip to level and lift the board after the pop. Prerequisites: Pop. Unlocks: ollie height, the leveling motion every flip trick reuses. [MOVEMENT] Level Shoulders Keeping shoulders square and level over the board so the board stays level in the air. Prerequisites: none — train it from the first ollie. Unlocks: clean ollies, all flip tricks (prevents the board going crooked). Coaching cues (Rookie Mistakes): keep the back under 45° — hinge from the knees , not the waist (the hidden fault: people bend their knees by folding the back forward and don't realize it). Look at your feet/board to build the neural map and catch bad foot placement — even advanced skaters watch the board 70–80% of the time, glancing at the obstacle only to set the angle. [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation (Wind-and-Unwind) The continuous shoulder wind/unwind that drives every rotational trick. Wind the shoulders opposite the turn, then unwind in one continuous motion — pop at the exact moment the shoulders are parallel over the board and you're at full squat. The killer error: winding, then stopping rotation to pop, then resuming — that kills the trick. Prerequisites: Pop, Level Shoulders. Unlocks: fs/bs 180, 360s, bigspin, half/full cab — every rotational trick. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #02. Don't copy pros' late rotation look as a beginner — that's years of muscle memory.) [DRILL] Stationary Ollies Over a Stick Pop ollies standing still, clearing a low stick/rail to force height and front-foot drag. Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide. Unlocks: rolling ollie, obstacle clearance confidence. [TRICK] Stationary Ollie Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide, Level Shoulders. Unlocks: Rolling Ollie. [TRICK] Rolling Ollie Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Stationary Ollie, rolling comfort, [SKILL] Commitment. Unlocks: Ollie up a Curb, all flip tricks (a flip trick is a manipulated rolling ollie). [TRICK] Ollie up a Curb Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie. Unlocks: Ollie over an Obstacle, ledge approaches (grinds line). [TRICK] Ollie over an Obstacle Discipline: flatground, gap Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Stationary Ollies Over a Stick (drill transfer). Unlocks: Ollie down Stairs/Gap, [SKILL] Terrain Reading at speed. [TRICK] Ollie down Stairs / Gap Discipline: gap Prerequisites: Ollie over an Obstacle, [SKILL] Commitment (fear management). Unlocks: ollie onto/off objects, flip tricks down gaps, the whole send it tier. Flip Tricks All flip tricks are a [TRICK] Rolling Ollie plus a flick. They all share Pop , Level Shoulders , and a Catch . [MOVEMENT] Catch Stopping the board's rotation/flip with the feet and riding away — the difference between a land and a credit. Prerequisites: Pop, board awareness in the air. Unlocks: clean kickflips, heelflips, every flip trick's makeable version. Coaching cues (Rookie Mistakes): land on toes + ball of foot , then squat to absorb — ankle → knee → hips work as shocks. Landing on heels removes the ankle as a shock point and your joints eat the impact (heel bruises, hot pockets ). Suck knees to chest for height and clearance — needs hip-flexor mobility (tight flexors are why beginners can't tuck). (Mined: #20, #13.) [MOVEMENT] Flick (Kick Flick) The toe/ankle snap off the corner of the nose that spins the board on its long axis. Prerequisites: Pop, Front-Foot Slide. Unlocks: kickflip , varial kickflip , 360 flip . [MOVEMENT] Heel Flick The heel snap off the toe-side edge that flips the board the opposite way. Prerequisites: Pop, Level Shoulders. Unlocks: heelflip , varial heelflip . [DRILL] Primo Flicks / Flip-and-Catch Stationary: flip the board with the flick and catch it with feet, no air, to learn rotation speed and catch timing. Prerequisites: Flick or Heel Flick. Unlocks: Kickflip, Heelflip (builds the catch without committing to the pop). [TRICK] Kickflip Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Flick, Catch, Level Shoulders; Primo Flicks drill. Shared movements: Pop + Level Shoulders + Catch (same as Heelflip). Unlocks: Varial Kickflip, 360 Flip, kickflip-into-manual, kickflip down stairs. [TRICK] Heelflip Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Heel Flick, Catch, Level Shoulders. Shared movements: Pop + Level Shoulders + Catch (same as Kickflip — that's why landing one accelerates the other). Unlocks: Varial Heelflip, heelflip variations. [TRICK] Frontside Flip A frontside 180 and a kickflip at the same time — the board flips while you and it rotate 180° frontside. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Kickflip, fs 180 (ollie), [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Catch. Shared movements: Flick + Shoulder Rotation (wind-and-unwind) + Catch. Unlocks: fs flip variations, the frontside flip-trick family. (Blind catch — you rotate away from the board, so committing the shoulders + spotting the catch late is the crux.) [TRICK] Backside Flip A backside 180 and a kickflip at once — the board flips while you and it rotate 180° backside. Mirror of the frontside flip, but the catch is visible (you rotate toward the board), so spotting the landing is more forgiving than the fs flip's blind catch. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Kickflip, bs 180 (ollie), [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Catch. Shared movements: Flick + Shoulder Rotation (wind-and-unwind) + Catch. Unlocks: bs flip variations, the backside flip-trick family. Where Tiago is: 2/10 — early, landing the occasional one. Long road but it's started. [TRICK] Varial Kickflip Kickflip + backside pop shuvit rotation at once. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Kickflip, Pop Shuvit. Unlocks: Varial Heelflip, hardflip, 360 flip approaches. [TRICK] 360 Flip (Tre Flip) Kickflip + 360 shuvit scoop together — a peak intermediate trick. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Kickflip, 360 Shuvit, strong Scoop. Unlocks: advanced flip combos, the you've arrived intermediate milestone. Rotational / Shuvit Tricks [MOVEMENT] Scoop The back-foot scrape/whip that spins the board flat (horizontal axis). Power source for all shuvit-family tricks. Prerequisites: Roll-and-Balance, back-foot control. Unlocks: shuvit , pop shuvit , 360 shuvit , bigspin , and the rotation half of varial kickflip / 360 flip . [TRICK] Shuvit (Pop-less / Ground Shuvit) Board spins 180° flat under you, no pop. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Scoop, Roll-and-Balance. Unlocks: Pop Shuvit. [TRICK] Pop Shuvit Shuvit with an ollie pop so the board spins in the air. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Shuvit, Pop, Catch. Shared movements: Pop + Scoop. Unlocks: Frontside Pop Shuvit, 360 Shuvit, Varial Kickflip, Bigspin. [TRICK] Frontside Pop Shuvit Pop shuvit spun the harder (frontside) direction. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Pop Shuvit. Unlocks: frontside flip variations, FS bigspin. [TRICK] 360 Shuvit Full 360° board rotation, no flip. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Pop Shuvit, strong Scoop. Unlocks: Bigspin, 360 Flip (the rotation half of the tre). [TRICK] Bigspin 360 shuvit + 180 body rotation together. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: 360 Shuvit, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Alignment (body wind-up). Unlocks: bigspin flip, half-cab/cab body-rotation feel (stance line). Manuals Balance [MOVEMENT] Weight Shift Controlled fore/aft shift over the wheels — the core of balance tricks and tic-tac. Prerequisites: rolling comfort. Unlocks: Tic-Tac, Manual, Nose Manual. [DRILL] Manual Rolls Roll into a wheelie, hold the balance point a few feet, set down clean. Repeat for distance. Prerequisites: Weight Shift, Roll-and-Balance. Unlocks: Manual, Nose Manual. [TRICK] Manual Rolling wheelie on the back wheels, held for distance. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Weight Shift, Manual Rolls drill. Unlocks: Nose Manual, Manual the length of a box, ollie-into-manual, flip-into/out-of-manual combos. [TRICK] Nose Manual Wheelie on the front wheels — harder balance point. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Manual. Unlocks: nose-manual combos, ledge manual lines. [TRICK] Manual the Length of a Box Holding a manual the full length of a manny pad. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Manual, [SKILL] Commitment. Unlocks: in/out combos ( ollie → manual → kickflip out ), the manual-tricks lane in lines. Grinds Slides (intro) [SKILL] Terrain Reading Judging ledge height, speed needed, run-up, and wax before you commit. Prerequisites: Ollie over an Obstacle, real session mileage. Unlocks: safe grind/slide approaches, gap selection, every spot decision. [MOVEMENT] Ollie-to-Lock Ollieing onto an edge and landing locked into a grind/slide position. Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Level Shoulders, Terrain Reading. Unlocks: 50-50, boardslide, all ledge tricks. [TRICK] 50-50 Grind Both trucks grinding a ledge/rail. Discipline: ledge, rail Prerequisites: Ollie up a Curb, Ollie-to-Lock, Terrain Reading. Unlocks: 5-0 grind, nosegrind, crooked grind. [TRICK] Boardslide Sliding on the board's underside across an obstacle. Discipline: ledge, rail Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Ollie-to-Lock, [SKILL] Commitment. Unlocks: lipslide, tailslide, the slide family. [TRICK] Noseslide Sliding on the underside of the nose across a ledge, board perpendicular. Discipline: ledge Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Ollie-to-Lock, nose control, [SKILL] Terrain Reading. Unlocks: Switch Noseslide, tailslide, noseslide variations (nollie-in, shuv-out). (Tiago owns this BS regular.) [TRICK] Switch Noseslide Noseslide in switch stance. Discipline: ledge Prerequisites: Noseslide (regular), Switch Ollie, [SKILL] Switch Competency. Unlocks: switch ledge lines, the switch slide family. (Target — never landed yet.) [TRICK] Switch 50-50 50-50 grind in switch stance. Discipline: ledge, rail Prerequisites: 50-50 Grind (regular), Switch Ollie, [SKILL] Switch Competency. Unlocks: switch grind family, switch ledge lines. (Target — never landed yet; owns FS 50-50, switch is the stance gap.) [TRICK] Crooked Grind (FS) A nosegrind cocked across the ledge — grinding on the front truck with the nose angled into the ledge. Discipline: ledge Prerequisites: 50-50 Grind, nose-lock / nosegrind feel, Ollie-to-Lock, [SKILL] Terrain Reading. Unlocks: crooked variations, the nose-grind ledge family. (Front-truck-only balance is the crux — the nose wants to slip; committing forward into the ledge holds the lock. Target — never landed yet.) Transition Basics [TRICK] Drop-in Committing from the coping into the ramp. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: rolling comfort, [SKILL] Commitment (this is 90% fear). Unlocks: all ramp/bowl skating, pumping for speed, stalls. [TRICK] Kickturn on Transition Pivoting on the transition wall to come back down. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Kickturn (flat), Drop-in, Pumping. Unlocks: carving lines, higher kickturns, stall setups. [TRICK] Fakie Rock / Rock to Fakie Rolling up, stalling the front trucks over the coping, coming back fakie. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Drop-in, Pumping, [SKILL] Fakie comfort. Unlocks: rock 'n' roll, axle stalls, coping tricks. [TRICK] Axle Stall (50-50 Stall) Stalling both trucks on the coping, then re-entering. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Kickturn on Transition, Fakie Rock. Unlocks: Backside Grind (transition), disasters, the coping-trick family. [TRICK] Backside Disaster Ollie/tap the tail over the coping so the board lands rocked on the lip (front truck over, deck on the coping), then roll back in fakie. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Axle Stall, Rock to Fakie, [SKILL] Commitment. Unlocks: disaster variations, the coping-trick family. (The crux is committing the tap over the lip and trusting the rock — half-rolled-away means the commitment isn't all the way there yet.) [TRICK] Backside / Frontside Grind (Transition) Grinding the coping along the transition (not just stalling on it). Backside (back to the ramp) is the standard first grind; frontside (facing out) is harder — the lock and the re-entry are both blinder. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Axle Stall, Kickturn on Transition, Pumping (for grind speed). Unlocks: longer grinds, grind-to-stall combos, the coping-trick family. Where Tiago is: BS — stalls 6/10, grind-through not yet. FS — 2/10, just starting to lock. [TRICK] Frontside Tailstall (Transition) Ride up the transition, stall the tail on the coping (board pointing up/out), then drop back in. Discipline: transition Prerequisites: Drop-in, Kickturn on Transition, Pumping, [SKILL] Fakie Comfort (for the re-entry). Unlocks: tailblock stalls, tailstall-to-fakie, the lip-trick family. Practice note: grind reps on a low wall — IAPI's smaller mini ramp / small quarter (close-in, convenient) or ORLA's lowest transitions (every height available). Low consequence + high rep count is exactly how a stall trick gets owned. See SPOTS.md . (Target — new goal.) Stance Expansion (fakie / switch / nollie) [SKILL] Switch Competency Skating your unnatural stance as if it were normal — doubles your whole trick list. Prerequisites: solid regular-stance ollie + a flip trick. Unlocks: every switch trick; full-cab; the second skater inside you. [TRICK] Fakie Ollie Ollie while rolling backwards. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, [SKILL] Fakie comfort. Unlocks: Half-Cab, fakie flip tricks. [TRICK] Nollie Ollie off the nose while rolling forward. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Level Shoulders, nose-pop feel (from Nose Manual / Tic-Tac). Unlocks: Nollie Frontside 180, nollie flip tricks, nollie line. [TRICK] Nollie Frontside 180 Nollie with a 180° frontside body+board rotation. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Nollie, fs 180 feel, [MOVEMENT] Shoulder Rotation, Level Shoulders. Unlocks: nollie fs flip, the nollie rotation line. Where Tiago is: 7/10 but feels weird every rep — high make-rate masking a mechanic fault (likely an early shoulder push, or popping off the toe instead of clean off the nose). Film it: the fix is probably one cue, and it's what's capping it below ✅. [TRICK] Switch Ollie Rolling ollie in switch stance. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Rolling Ollie, Switch Competency (developing). Unlocks: switch flip tricks, switch manuals, doubling the flip-trick lane. [TRICK] Half-Cab Fakie 180 ollie (half a caballerial). Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Fakie Ollie, Shoulder Alignment. Unlocks: Full Cab, half-cab flip. [TRICK] Full Cab (Caballerial) Fakie 360 ollie. Discipline: flatground Prerequisites: Half-Cab, Bigspin body-rotation feel. Unlocks: cab flips, advanced fakie line. Recurring Movements (reference) These are the shared mechanics. Master a movement once and every trick that lists it gets cheaper. Find the parent move you already own; isolate the single mechanic that's different; drill that. 2. Reverse-engineer from your own bails. The kickflip came from Mullen watching how the board flipped when his front foot slipped off during a failed ollie — then deliberately timing that flip after the pop. Your bails are showing you the mechanic of your next trick. Film them; don't just dismiss them. 3. One mechanical reframe often unlocks a stuck trick. The impossible only worked once he treated his back foot as a fixed axle for the board to wrap around — not more force, a different model of what the foot is doing. When a trick fights you, try re-describing what one body part is for , not trying harder. 4. Chase generative primitives, not dead-end novelties. Some tricks are platforms that open a whole category (ollie, kickflip — a key that opened the door to an unlimited number of new tricks ); others are awkward one-offs (Mullen on the impossible: a more awkward trick with limitations ). Before grinding a trick, ask what does owning this unlock? Spend reps where the Unlocks edge is richest. This is the map-level version of building the shuvit and flip lines in parallel ( WISDOM #8 ). 5. Constraints are design prompts. Tony Hawk invented ollie-into-airs-and-grab-late precisely because he lacked the mass to blast airs the normal way. A physical limitation is a reason to find a different technique, not evidence you can't do the trick. 6. Verbalize the mechanics. Mullen's coaches made him break each new trick into repeatable steps out loud until the picture was clear. Saying the sub-movements exposes the gap — it's a learning tool on top of filming.

sections.title: Wisdom
sections.html:
Wisdom — What Actually Moves the Needle

The brain of the system. Everything in SESSIONS.md is built to apply these. Every JOURNAL entry should either add a principle here or sharpen one.

This file is leverage-ranked, not chronological. The top of the list is where the smallest deliberate change produces the biggest long-term gain. Each principle is tagged with why it has leverage so I don't lose the reasoning.

The core finding (from both a systems-thinking pass and a first-principles pass — they converged, which is the signal): the inputs intermediates fight hardest over — more hours, better gear — are the weakest leverage points. The highest-leverage inputs are nearly free and mostly cognitive. Volume amplifies structure; it doesn't replace it.

The Four Compounding Inputs (highest leverage first)

1. Film and review every session, on the spot

Leverage — feedback delay (the master loop). A rep you didn't see is a coin flip on whether it helped or hurt; only observed, corrected reps encode the motor pattern. Filming closes the feedback delay and converts blind reps into learning reps. This is the one that makes every other input work — without it, more practice can groove the wrong pattern.

  • How: phone on a bag/cone, film attempts in batches, review between tries. Look for one thing per trick (shoulders? early flick? bail timing?).
  • Tell: if I can't say why the last attempt failed, I'm not practicing, I'm gambling.

2. Redefine "got it" to 8/10 both stances, landed in a line

Leverage — the goal of the system (reroutes all hours). "Land it once" is a fluke; one landing reroutes nothing. Redefining success as a percentage in both stances inside a line reroutes every hour from collecting tricks to owning them — which is what actually makes me look and feel like a better skater.

  • How: a trick isn't "learned" until ~8/10 reliable, attempted regular and at least started switch/fakie, and able to roll away into the next move.
  • Warning: expect a 2–3 week stretch where trick count stops climbing after adopting this. That's consistency debt being paid down, not a plateau.
  • Sharpen (Mullen): the true marker isn't the landing, it's automaticity — I've stopped thinking mid-trick and can improvise off it ("the unity of board and skater," doing nothing yet in total control). Add to the test: can I roll into the next move from it, and could I do the line eyes-closed? And remember invent-to-own is years, not days — he invented the impossible in 1982 and only trusted it in a contest run in 1986.

3. Block-practice the substrate, 15 min every session

Leverage — raise the floor under every trick at once. The sub-movements (pop, balance, rolling speed, weak stance) cap the ceiling of everything built on them. Fifteen focused minutes on the substrate lifts the floor under the entire trick map simultaneously — far better ROI than the same minutes on one hard trick.

  • The substrate menu: pop height (stationary ollies over a stick), manuals (balance), rolling speed/commitment, and weak-stance reps of tricks I already own.
  • The cheat code: weak-stance work roughly doubles my vocabulary from skills I already have (specificity applied to owned skills). Cheapest progress available.

4. Build a fear-exposure ladder

Leverage — drain the avoidance loop that gates whole categories. Commitment is the #1 intermediate limiter, and fear is a separate trainable system from motor skill — it responds to graded exposure, not to getting better at the trick. Bigger gaps, rails, drop-ins stay locked not because the body can't but because the bail-out fires. A ladder drains that loop directly.

  • How: one controlled scary rep per session at the edge of comfort (slightly bigger set, commit to the boardslide, drop the bigger ramp). Small, deliberate, every time.
  • Tell: most of my failed attempts on "hard" tricks are incomplete commitment (bailing), not lack of ability. Those are fear reps, not skill reps.

Supporting Principles (still high-value)

5. Build the movement, not the trick

When a trick fights me, the missing piece is almost always a sub-[MOVEMENT] or a [SKILL] (usually commitment), not "more attempts at the trick." Trying the whole trick with a missing movement just grooves failure. Diagnose down the graph in TRICKS.md, drill the movement, climb back up.

6. Specificity — rehearse the real conditions

Skill is context-bound (SAID). Stationary kickflips ≠ rolling ≠ down-stairs. Practice the stance, speed, and terrain I actually want to perform in. Don't expect flat-ground reps to transfer fully to the gap.

7. Respect consolidation — end on a make

Motor memory locks in during rest and sleep, not during slams. An over-fatigued, fear-saturated session encodes degraded patterns. Quit while ahead — end the session (or each trick block) on a clean make, manage slam-fatigue, sleep on it.

8. Build the shuvit line in parallel with the flip line

Not after. varial kickflip and 360 flip are just the kickflip Flick + the shuvit Scoop. Growing both lanes together means the hard combos arrive almost for free instead of being a wall later. (See the transfer insight in TRICKS.md.)

9. Volume comes last, not first

Keep skating a lot — but only after the four compounding inputs point my hours at the right loops. Hours are real (you need mileage), they're just the weakest leverage point. Don't substitute volume for structure.

  • Adherence tactic (Norman Woods): people massively overestimate how much they actually skate vs. sit. Audit the real skate:sit ratio at a session and nudge it 5 min at a time, rather than forcing a big change.
  • Sharpen (Mullen): presence is the active ingredient, not minutes. Mullen skated "four hours a day, but in an empty way, like some robot that can't stop" and it built nothing. Engaged reps compound; automated reps just burn the body. Also: skills rot week-over-week without maintenance reps — a layoff doesn't freeze my level, it decays it.

10. Remove equipment variables before blaming technique

Loose trucks rock under your squat and the urethane (bushings, pivot cups, wheels) eats the force you meant to put into the board — Norman Woods: "cuts your pop in half or worse." Before grinding away at a trick, tighten trucks to medium so the board stops stealing your power. Cheap, instant, and it stops you training against your own setup. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #08.)

  • Sharpen (Mullen): when you do change setup deliberately (wider deck, looser trucks, new shape), migrate millimeter-by-millimeter over weeks, not in one jump — let the body recalibrate to a single tiny change at a time. Ternasky widened Mullen's board 1mm/week until he was on a normal street deck. Pairs with #13 (change one variable).

11. Train like an athlete or you won't get to train

Warm up by repping the trick on smooth flat ground before committing to stairs/rails (most injuries come from showing up cold and shredding). Stretch — hip flexors especially, since tight ones stop you sucking knees to chest. Cross-train. Injury-proofing isn't separate from progress; it's the prerequisite for volume (#9) and the substrate block (#3). (Mined: Rookie Mistake #09, #13.)

  • Sharpen (Mullen): lazy reps injure — commit fully even to trivial ones. The worst break of his career came from doing an easy 360 flip half-heartedly with his back foot off — "I did it lazily… my fibula was snapped." No slam, no big set; just an uncommitted rep on a trick he owned. And the opposite extreme is just as dangerous: ever-rising intensity with no recovery (daily mile-PRs on 3 hours' sleep, near-anorexia) nearly killed him. Commit fully and cap the intensity.

12. Ego/risk gate — capacity ≠ opportunity

What I can do and what this spot / day / board / energy will let me land are unrelated. Refuse bad-EV spots: crusty ground, worn deck, time pressure, an amped nervous system. Only the trick and how it looked is ever remembered — never how bad the spot was. Koston rule: feel a spot within ~5 minutes or walk away. This is the inverse of the fear-ladder (#4) — that one trains exposure; this one trains restraint. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #14.)

13. Adaptation loop — change a variable, not the attempt

If a trick isn't landing after honest reps, I'm doing it wrong for my body — repeating the same attempt just trains the wrong pattern. Vary one thing between tries: foot placement, approach angle, stance, pop height, or truck tightness. And copy my own body, not a pro 10 years and a different build ahead of me. Pairs with #5 (build the movement) and #1 (film to see which variable is off). (Mined: Rookie Mistake #15.)

14. Juggle disciplines by dependency and consequence — not by calendar

Leverage — stops the calendar (and my context-switching) from fragmenting progress across terrains. The four disciplines — flatground, transition, ledge/rail, gap — are not four equal parallel lanes. They have a dependency order and a consequence cost, and ignoring either is how progress scatters thin across all of them.

  • Flatground is the substrate, not a lane. A flip/ollie has to be near-effortless before it survives a ledge or a set (SAID, #6). So flatground is the daily anchor (#3) — never "today's discipline."
  • Consequence sets frequency. Flat + transition are low-consequence → high-frequency, skate them almost daily. Ledge is medium. Rail/gap are high-consequence and high-impact → low-frequency, and only ever early in a session on fresh legs (#11, #12) — never tired, never the closer.
  • Let the spot pick the primary, not a schedule. Rigid one-discipline-per-day and week-long single-focus both fail: rigidity kills the stoke that keeps me consistent, and skating is opportunistic. Anchor on flatground every session, then let the day's spot + body + crew choose the primary — that's what SPOTS.md is for.
  • ≤2–3 frontier tricks across ALL disciplines at once. The brain consolidates slowly; more than ~3 active frontiers is just my context-switching failure wearing a disguise. Everything else is maintenance and lines. (Sharpens SESSIONS' "2 primaries max" — that's per session; this is the cap across the whole week.)
  • The full method + weekly shape: SESSIONS.md → Juggling the four disciplines. Current grounded frontier picks: ASSESSMENT.md → Discipline frontier map.

From Rodney Mullen — The Mutt (2026-06-26 digest, see JOURNAL)

The most methodical skater who ever lived treated practice as engineering: a notebook, a report card, tricks dissected into primitives. These five earned their place because they each change what I do, not just what I know.

15. A plateau is a missing principle, not a rep deficit

Leverage — stops months of wasted volume. When a trick won't budge after honest reps, the bottleneck is almost never "more attempts" — it's one sub-skill I haven't found yet. Grinding volume against a missing principle just grooves the failure. Stop repping, start diagnosing (pairs with #5 and #13).

  • The story: Mullen was stuck at five and a half 360 spins for months, then "found his center" and jumped to ~20 within a week. The months were wasted hunting reps instead of the principle.
  • Tell: if I've put in the reps and a trick is flat-lining, the next session is a diagnosis session (film, drop down the graph, change one variable) — not another volume session.

16. Invent by recombination — and prefer generative primitives

Leverage — turns owned skills into new tricks for nearly free. A "new" trick is usually an owned movement ported to a new plane or stance, plus one diagnosed fix — not something learned whole from scratch. And primitives are not equal: some are platforms that open a whole category, others are awkward dead-ends. Spend reps on the generative ones.

  • The story: the flatground ollie was vert's tail-scoop ported to flat + dragging the front foot to level the "seesaw"; the kickflip was reverse-engineered from how the board flipped during a bail. He prized the ollie and kickflip for the doors they opened, not the trick itself.
  • How: watch my own bails for the next trick's mechanic; ask "what does this primitive unlock?" before grinding it. Mechanics + worked examples in TRICKS.md → Trick Invention & Design.

17. Mastery doesn't transfer; vocabulary does

Leverage — sets the right expectation when I cross disciplines (the antidote to context-switch frustration). Crossing from flatground to transition/ledge/gap, I am a true beginner on the discipline-specific skills — speed, distance judgment, fear — even while expert on the board. The thing that does carry is the primitive vocabulary, and it's what makes the climb fast.

  • The story: the best freestyler alive ran into curbs for a year learning street ("I looked like I had no depth perception") — yet his freestyle kickflips became the literal foundation of technical street skating.
  • How: when a new discipline humbles me, that's expected, not regression. Lean on owned primitives as substrate; train the discipline-specific perceptual skills (e.g. landing-zone calibration) as their own thing. Sharpens #14 and #6.

18. Playing it safe is its own failure mode

Leverage — the performance-side complement to the fear ladder (#4). #4 fights under-commitment in learning; this fights over-caution in performance. Optimizing a session to not-fail (only owned tricks, never the edge) strips out exactly the reps that raise my level — and it feels hollow.

  • The story: Mullen won contests on safe runs and "felt like a fraud"; his skating only broke through to Skater of the Year once he skated for the trick he wanted, ignoring the score.
  • Tell: if a whole session had zero attempts I might not land, I optimized for the wrong thing. Skate for the trick, not the tally.

19. Protect the play, or the engine seizes

Leverage — guards the fuel under everything else. Relentless outcome-intensity burns the engine out (Mullen: panic attacks, near-anorexia, "skating in an empty way"). The skaters who lasted "treated skating like a toy." Volume (#9) only compounds if the desire to skate survives — and skating is my most consistently-alive creative spark, so systematizing the joy out of it would defeat the whole point of this system.

  • Tell: if the system ever makes me dread a session, the system is wrong, not me. Cut structure, go play, end on a make.

The one model to carry

Only filmed reps of the right fundamentals, practiced to true ownership, compound.

Everything else is amplification.

Open questions / to sharpen

(Add via JOURNAL as I learn. These are where the wisdom is still thin.)

  • What's my actual make-percentage threshold for "owned"? (Calibrate against real footage.)
  • Best fear-ladder rungs for my specific locked categories (rails? bigger sets?).
  • Warm-up sequence that primes pop + balance fastest.
sections.text: Wisdom — What Actually Moves the Needle The brain of the system. Everything in SESSIONS.md is built to apply these. Every JOURNAL entry should either add a principle here or sharpen one. This file is leverage-ranked , not chronological. The top of the list is where the smallest deliberate change produces the biggest long-term gain. Each principle is tagged with why it has leverage so I don't lose the reasoning. The core finding (from both a systems-thinking pass and a first-principles pass — they converged, which is the signal): the inputs intermediates fight hardest over — more hours, better gear — are the weakest leverage points. The highest-leverage inputs are nearly free and mostly cognitive. Volume amplifies structure; it doesn't replace it. The Four Compounding Inputs (highest leverage first) 1. Film and review every session, on the spot Leverage — feedback delay (the master loop). A rep you didn't see is a coin flip on whether it helped or hurt; only observed, corrected reps encode the motor pattern. Filming closes the feedback delay and converts blind reps into learning reps. This is the one that makes every other input work — without it, more practice can groove the wrong pattern. How: phone on a bag/cone, film attempts in batches, review between tries. Look for one thing per trick (shoulders? early flick? bail timing?). Tell: if I can't say why the last attempt failed, I'm not practicing, I'm gambling. 2. Redefine got it to 8/10 both stances, landed in a line Leverage — the goal of the system (reroutes all hours). Land it once is a fluke; one landing reroutes nothing. Redefining success as a percentage in both stances inside a line reroutes every hour from collecting tricks to owning them — which is what actually makes me look and feel like a better skater. How: a trick isn't learned until ~8/10 reliable, attempted regular and at least started switch/fakie, and able to roll away into the next move. Warning: expect a 2–3 week stretch where trick count stops climbing after adopting this. That's consistency debt being paid down, not a plateau. Sharpen (Mullen): the true marker isn't the landing, it's automaticity — I've stopped thinking mid-trick and can improvise off it ( the unity of board and skater, doing nothing yet in total control). Add to the test: can I roll into the next move from it, and could I do the line eyes-closed? And remember invent-to-own is years, not days — he invented the impossible in 1982 and only trusted it in a contest run in 1986. 3. Block-practice the substrate, 15 min every session Leverage — raise the floor under every trick at once. The sub-movements (pop, balance, rolling speed, weak stance) cap the ceiling of everything built on them. Fifteen focused minutes on the substrate lifts the floor under the entire trick map simultaneously — far better ROI than the same minutes on one hard trick. The substrate menu: pop height (stationary ollies over a stick), manuals (balance), rolling speed/commitment, and weak-stance reps of tricks I already own. The cheat code: weak-stance work roughly doubles my vocabulary from skills I already have (specificity applied to owned skills). Cheapest progress available. 4. Build a fear-exposure ladder Leverage — drain the avoidance loop that gates whole categories. Commitment is the #1 intermediate limiter, and fear is a separate trainable system from motor skill — it responds to graded exposure , not to getting better at the trick. Bigger gaps, rails, drop-ins stay locked not because the body can't but because the bail-out fires. A ladder drains that loop directly. How: one controlled scary rep per session at the edge of comfort (slightly bigger set, commit to the boardslide, drop the bigger ramp). Small, deliberate, every time. Tell: most of my failed attempts on hard tricks are incomplete commitment (bailing), not lack of ability. Those are fear reps, not skill reps. Supporting Principles (still high-value) 5. Build the movement, not the trick When a trick fights me, the missing piece is almost always a sub- [MOVEMENT] or a [SKILL] (usually commitment), not more attempts at the trick. Trying the whole trick with a missing movement just grooves failure. Diagnose down the graph in TRICKS.md , drill the movement, climb back up. 6. Specificity — rehearse the real conditions Skill is context-bound (SAID). Stationary kickflips ≠ rolling ≠ down-stairs. Practice the stance, speed, and terrain I actually want to perform in. Don't expect flat-ground reps to transfer fully to the gap. 7. Respect consolidation — end on a make Motor memory locks in during rest and sleep, not during slams. An over-fatigued, fear-saturated session encodes degraded patterns. Quit while ahead — end the session (or each trick block) on a clean make, manage slam-fatigue, sleep on it. 8. Build the shuvit line in parallel with the flip line Not after. varial kickflip and 360 flip are just the kickflip Flick + the shuvit Scoop . Growing both lanes together means the hard combos arrive almost for free instead of being a wall later. (See the transfer insight in TRICKS.md .) 9. Volume comes last, not first Keep skating a lot — but only after the four compounding inputs point my hours at the right loops. Hours are real (you need mileage), they're just the weakest leverage point. Don't substitute volume for structure. Adherence tactic (Norman Woods): people massively overestimate how much they actually skate vs. sit. Audit the real skate:sit ratio at a session and nudge it 5 min at a time, rather than forcing a big change. Sharpen (Mullen): presence is the active ingredient, not minutes. Mullen skated four hours a day, but in an empty way, like some robot that can't stop and it built nothing. Engaged reps compound; automated reps just burn the body. Also: skills rot week-over-week without maintenance reps — a layoff doesn't freeze my level, it decays it. 10. Remove equipment variables before blaming technique Loose trucks rock under your squat and the urethane (bushings, pivot cups, wheels) eats the force you meant to put into the board — Norman Woods: cuts your pop in half or worse. Before grinding away at a trick, tighten trucks to medium so the board stops stealing your power. Cheap, instant, and it stops you training against your own setup. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #08.) Sharpen (Mullen): when you do change setup deliberately (wider deck, looser trucks, new shape), migrate millimeter-by-millimeter over weeks , not in one jump — let the body recalibrate to a single tiny change at a time. Ternasky widened Mullen's board 1mm/week until he was on a normal street deck. Pairs with #13 (change one variable). 11. Train like an athlete or you won't get to train Warm up by repping the trick on smooth flat ground before committing to stairs/rails (most injuries come from showing up cold and shredding). Stretch — hip flexors especially, since tight ones stop you sucking knees to chest. Cross-train. Injury-proofing isn't separate from progress; it's the prerequisite for volume (#9) and the substrate block (#3). (Mined: Rookie Mistake #09, #13.) Sharpen (Mullen): lazy reps injure — commit fully even to trivial ones. The worst break of his career came from doing an easy 360 flip half-heartedly with his back foot off — I did it lazily… my fibula was snapped. No slam, no big set; just an uncommitted rep on a trick he owned. And the opposite extreme is just as dangerous: ever-rising intensity with no recovery (daily mile-PRs on 3 hours' sleep, near-anorexia) nearly killed him. Commit fully and cap the intensity. 12. Ego/risk gate — capacity ≠ opportunity What I can do and what this spot / day / board / energy will let me land are unrelated. Refuse bad-EV spots: crusty ground, worn deck, time pressure, an amped nervous system. Only the trick and how it looked is ever remembered — never how bad the spot was. Koston rule: feel a spot within ~5 minutes or walk away. This is the inverse of the fear-ladder (#4) — that one trains exposure; this one trains restraint. (Mined: Rookie Mistake #14.) 13. Adaptation loop — change a variable, not the attempt If a trick isn't landing after honest reps, I'm doing it wrong for my body — repeating the same attempt just trains the wrong pattern. Vary one thing between tries: foot placement, approach angle, stance, pop height, or truck tightness. And copy my own body, not a pro 10 years and a different build ahead of me. Pairs with #5 (build the movement) and #1 (film to see which variable is off). (Mined: Rookie Mistake #15.) 14. Juggle disciplines by dependency and consequence — not by calendar Leverage — stops the calendar (and my context-switching) from fragmenting progress across terrains. The four disciplines — flatground, transition, ledge/rail, gap — are not four equal parallel lanes. They have a dependency order and a consequence cost, and ignoring either is how progress scatters thin across all of them. Flatground is the substrate, not a lane. A flip/ollie has to be near-effortless before it survives a ledge or a set (SAID, #6). So flatground is the daily anchor (#3) — never today's discipline. Consequence sets frequency. Flat + transition are low-consequence → high-frequency, skate them almost daily. Ledge is medium. Rail/gap are high-consequence and high-impact → low-frequency, and only ever early in a session on fresh legs (#11, #12) — never tired, never the closer. Let the spot pick the primary, not a schedule. Rigid one-discipline-per-day and week-long single-focus both fail: rigidity kills the stoke that keeps me consistent, and skating is opportunistic. Anchor on flatground every session, then let the day's spot + body + crew choose the primary — that's what SPOTS.md is for. ≤2–3 frontier tricks across ALL disciplines at once. The brain consolidates slowly; more than ~3 active frontiers is just my context-switching failure wearing a disguise. Everything else is maintenance and lines.
