sections.title: Assessment
sections.html:
Assessment — Current Skill Set

An honest record of where I am, wired to the nodes in TRICKS.md. Session planning (SESSIONS.md) starts from this. Reassess monthly (or after a breakthrough) by adding a new snapshot — never overwrite; the history is the progress.

Status legend

Status Meaning
✅ owned ~8/10 reliable, can roll away into a line (per WISDOM principle #2)
🔧 learning landed but inconsistent (<8/10), actively working
🎯 want the target — prerequisites mostly met, next up
⬜ not yet prerequisites not met; don't grind this yet
↔️ switch/fakie note the stance dimension separately

Current snapshot — 2026-06-24 (Tiago's real self-report)

Solid intermediate with a rare both-stance foundation — owns ollies, shuvits, and pop-shuvits switch and regular, FS and BS. Edges are concentrated in switch flips, the "almost-owned" rotation tricks, and BS ledge consistency.

Foundations

Node Regular Switch/Fakie
Pushing & Rolling ✅ ✅
Tic-Tac ✅ ✅
Kickturn (FS/BS) ✅ ✅
Pumping & Carving ✅ ✅

Ollie line

Node Regular Switch/Fakie
Stationary Ollie ✅ ✅
Rolling Ollie ✅ ✅
Ollie up a Curb ✅ ✅
Ollie over an Obstacle ✅ ~60cm ✅ ~30cm
Ollie down Stairs/Gap ✅ max 3 stairs 🔧 1–2 stairs

Flip tricks

Node Regular Switch/Fakie
Kickflip ✅ 🔧 lands a few
Heelflip ✅ ⬜
Frontside Flip 🎯 in progress ⬜
Varial Kickflip 🔧 lands sometimes ⬜
360 Flip (tre) ⬜ ⬜

Shuvit line (FS + BS both)

Node Regular Switch/Fakie
Shuvit ✅ FS+BS ✅ FS+BS
Pop Shuvit ✅ FS+BS ✅ FS+BS
360 Shuvit 🔧 lands sometimes ⬜
Bigspin BS 🔧 almost owned · FS ⬜ ⬜

Manuals & balance

Node Regular Switch/Fakie
Manual ✅ (manny pads) 🔧 holds balance, not landed on a pad yet
Nose Manual 🔧 landed a few on pads ⬜

Ledges

Node Status
50-50 ✅ FS · 🔧 BS (inconsistent) · ⬜ switch
Noseslide ✅ BS regular

Transition / mini-ramp

Node Status
Drop-in ✅ both stances
Kickturn on transition ✅ both stances
Mini-ramp 50-50 ✅ regular
Axle stall 🔧 landed a few (switch)
Rock 'n' roll / rock to fakie ✅ regular + switch
Backside Disaster 🔧 lands & rolls away ~50%

Rails / flatbar

Node Status
Flatbar Boardslide ✅ regular

Core skills (derived from the trick data above — confirm or adjust the numbers)

[SKILL] Rating Note
Board Control 4/5 strong — both-stance flips, shuvits, manuals
Commitment / Fear Management 3/5 does 3-stair ollies, flatbar slides, drop-ins both ways; ceiling is bigger sets / BS 50-50 consistency
Terrain Reading 3/5 comfortable on ledges, flatbar, mini-ramp
Switch Competency 3/5 genuinely good for intermediate — owns switch foundation + shuvits; switch kickflip emerging
Fakie Comfort 3/5 rock-to-fakie + transition both stances
Pumping & Carving 4/5 owned both stances

⚠️ The 6 ratings above are my derivation from your trick report, not your self-rating — adjust any that feel off.

Active focus — Tiago's current goals in progress (stated 2026-06-24)

(these are the 4 things sessions revolve around — straight from Tiago, not derived)

  1. Switch kickflip → ownership (🔧 lands a few). Gateway to the whole switch flip line; WISDOM #3 — weak-stance work on a skill you already own is the cheapest vocabulary you can buy.
  2. Frontside flips (🎯 in progress). Kickflip (✅) + FS 180 — opens the FS flip family.
  3. Backside bigspin → 8/10 (🔧 almost owned). WISDOM #2 — convert the fluke into an owned trick.
  4. Backside disaster (🔧 ~50% roll-away). Push to consistency; WISDOM #2 + #4 — a coping-trick commitment rep.

Snapshot history

(add a new dated block each reassessment; keep old ones — the diff is the progress)

  • 2026-06-23 — structure created, snapshot intentionally left blank for Tiago to fill with real skill levels (honesty over a guessed baseline).
  • 2026-06-24 — first real snapshot from Tiago's self-report. Solid intermediate, strong both-stance foundation. Core-skill numbers derived from trick data (pending his confirmation). Active focus set: switch kickflip, close almost-owned rotations, BS 50-50 consistency.

Snapshot — 2026-06-25 (2h flatground session)

What changed since last

  • Switch kickflip 🔧 (lands a few → 0 landed today, but easier & more natural): no make, but the feel is arriving before the make — commitment/catch wiring in. Stays 🔧; trending toward ✅. Don't force it.
  • Frontside Flip 🎯 (in progress → great adjustments, feels closer): no land yet, real motor-pattern improvement. Stays 🎯; next session opens warmer.
  • Bigspin BS 🔧 almost owned → 5/10 confirmed. Consistency is the only gap to 8/10, not the trick itself.
  • Backside Disaster 🔧 → 5/10 confirmed (~50% roll-away, holding steady). Needs more committed reps to climb.

    No status flips this session (nothing reached ✅), but all four active goals moved the right direction. A productive non-PR day.

New active focus

(unchanged — same 4 goals; switch kickflip is closest to a breakthrough)

Snapshot — 2026-06-25b (expanded self-report — more tricks + new goals)

Tiago volunteered a wider set of what he's working on and wants. Logged verbatim, mapped to the legend. Several are new nodes not yet in the trick map above (flagged ➕ — pending TRICKS.md graph add).

Landing but inconsistent (🔧)

Node Level Note
➕ Backside Flip 2/10 Early — landing occasionally. Long road, but it's started.
360 Shuvit (BS) 5/10 Quantifies the prior "lands sometimes." Halfway.
➕ Nollie Frontside (180) 7/10 but feels weird every time High make-rate, but the feel is off — likely a pop-timing or shoulder-alignment quirk. Worth filming: 7/10 that feels wrong usually means a compensating habit that caps it below ✅.
Axle stall (switch) 1/10 Barely — landed a couple. Confirms prior 🔧.
➕ FS grind (transition) 2/10 Just started locking in.
➕ BS (transition) stall 6/10, grind not yet Solid stall; the grind-through is the next layer.

Owned (✅)

Node Note
Noseslide (BS regular) Confirmed owned — matches the 06-24 snapshot.

Targets — not yet landed (🎯 want / ⬜)

Node Status Prereq read
➕ Switch Noseslide 🎯 Owns BS noseslide regular + strong switch foundation → prereqs met, it's a real next-up.
Switch 50-50 (ledge) 🎯 Owns FS 50-50; switch is the stance gap.
➕ FS Crooked grind 🎯 Needs nose-on-ledge control; FS 50-50 is the base.
➕ FS Tailstall (transition) 🎯 New goal. Prereqs (drop-in, kickturn-on-tranny, pumping) all owned. ORLA's very low transition is the ideal rep spot — low consequence, high volume.
Switch Manual on a pad 🔧 holds balance, not landed Balance is there — it's a commit-to-the-pad gap, not a balance gap.

These expand the goal pool but I did not auto-promote any into the 4 active-focus goals — confirmed 2026-06-25: active focus stays the original 4 (switch kickflip, FS flip, BS bigspin, BS disaster). The expanded list is the on-deck queue.

Discipline frontier map — 2026-06-26 (derived; grounds the ≤3-frontier call)

Maps the active focus + on-deck queue onto the four disciplines, per WISDOM #14 and SESSIONS → Juggling the disciplines. A derived recommendation, not a self-report — adjust any pick that feels off (same status as the core-skill ratings above).

The imbalance to see first: 3 of the 4 current active-focus goals are flatground/rotation (switch kickflip, FS flip, BS bigspin); only BS disaster is transition; ledge, rail, and gap have zero active frontiers. That's substrate-heavy — defensible, since flatground gates everything (#14) — but it under-uses the variety that keeps sessions fresh and leaves whole disciplines cold.

Discipline Frontier candidates (from active focus + on-deck) Grounded call
Flatground (substrate) switch kickflip 🔧 (closest to breakthrough) · FS flip 🎯 · BS bigspin 5/10 · nollie FS 180 7/10-but-weird Switch kickflip = THE flat frontier. FS flip rides along in the same blocks. BS bigspin is a convert (5→8/10 = maintenance, not frontier). Nollie FS 180 → film it: 7/10-that-feels-wrong usually caps below ✅.
Transition BS disaster 5/10 · FS tailstall 🎯 · FS/BS grind 2/10 One transition frontier — pick one, not both: BS disaster (push past 50%) or FS tailstall (ORLA/IAPI low tranny is built for it).
Ledge switch noseslide 🎯 (prereqs met) · switch 50-50 🎯 · FS crooked 🎯 Switch noseslide = the ledge frontier — owns BS noseslide regular + a strong switch base, so its prereqs are cleanest. The other two stay on-deck.
Rail / gap (none active) Deliberately not a frontier yet. Commitment skill is 3/5; keep these as the fear-ladder rep (#4), not a project block, until switch kickflip banks.

The 2–3 frontiers to actually run (the grounded pick):

  1. Switch kickflip — flatground; closest to ✅, gateway to the whole switch-flip line.
  2. BS disaster or FS tailstall — transition; choose one.
  3. (Optional 3rd) Switch noseslide — ledge; only if the first two aren't already eating the week.

This narrows the current flatground-heavy 4 into a discipline-balanced 2–3 — the WISDOM #14 cap, and a direct counter to the context-switching failure pattern. It's a proposal against the stated "active focus stays the original 4"; promote it only if it feels right.

sections.title: Session Planning
sections.html:
Sessions — Planning for Fast Progress

Where the system pays off. A good session isn't "go skate" — it's a structure that points my hours at the highest-leverage loops from WISDOM.md, aimed at my ASSESSMENT.md focus, navigating TRICKS.md.

The planning method (5 steps, ~3 min before skating)

  1. Read the focus. Open ASSESSMENT.md → "Active focus." That's the 2–4 things this session serves. Don't add more.
  2. Pick today's targets from the map. For each focus item, find its node in TRICKS.md. If it's a 🔧/🎯 trick, check its Prerequisites — if a prerequisite isn't ✅, today's real target is the prerequisite movement/drill, not the trick (WISDOM #5: build the movement, not the trick).
  3. Apply the four compounding inputs. Every session, non-negotiable: camera on (#1), substrate block (#3), one fear-ladder rep (#4), and "got it" = 8/10 both stances (#2).
  4. Time-box the blocks (see template). Warm-up → substrate → primary trick → fear rep → cool-down line.
  5. Set the end-on-a-make rule. Decide in advance what clean make ends the session (WISDOM #7 — protect consolidation).

Diagnostic shortcut when a trick won't come: drop down the graph. Trick → which [MOVEMENT] is failing? → which [DRILL] isolates it? Spend the block there, not on more failed whole-trick attempts.

Planning for leverage — what's actually worth a block

The point of a session isn't to touch the whole TRICKMATRIX — it's to spend a scarce ≤2h window on the few things that pay out most for long-term progress. Most of a good session is a fixed high-leverage scaffold; only a small slice is variable. Plan the slice well.

The fixed scaffold (always in — the highest-leverage inputs, WISDOM §1–4): film + review · 15-min substrate block (incl. weak-stance reps) · one fear-ladder rep · the 8/10-both-stances ownership standard · end on a make. That's ~half the session; you don't re-decide it each time.

The variable slice = 1–2 "primary" picks. Choose them by leverage, highest first:

Rank Pick this Why it pays out long-term WISDOM
1 A blocking shared [MOVEMENT] (pop, catch, scoop, shoulder rotation, balance) Fixing it cascades to dozens of tricks at once #3, #5
2 Weak-stance reps of an owned trick Nearly-free vocabulary doubling #3
3 Convert an almost-owned 🔧 (≈6–7/10 → 8/10) Cheapest ✅ you can buy; it then becomes a prerequisite and enters lines #2
4 A gateway/unlock trick (kickflip, pop shuvit, switch ollie, shoulder-rotation tricks) Opens a whole downstream family — beats dead-end trophy tricks #8
5 A 🎯 whose prerequisites are all ✅ Ready to invest; reps won't be wasted #5
6 A fear-gated category (rail, bigger set, commit) Drains the avoidance loop that locks whole categories #4

Every pick must serve an active-focus goal (ASSESSMENT) — 2 primaries max. Ruthless focus is itself your highest personal leverage: your one recurring failure pattern is context-switching, so a session that does two things well beats one that touches eight.

Skip / deprioritize (the answer to "don't keep the whole matrix on lock"):

  • ⬜ tricks whose prerequisites aren't met — grinding them grooves failure (#5); drill the missing movement instead.
  • Collecting new tricks (land-once) over owning a few (#2).
  • A hard trick when the real bottleneck is a movement — diagnose down the graph (#5).
  • Anything the spot / day / energy can't give a fair shot (#12 ego-risk gate).
  • More volume before the scaffold points the right way (#9).

So a planned session = fixed scaffold + (1–2 leverage primaries, fatigue-ordered). The matrix is the menu you pick from — never the to-do list.

Tool: bun Tools/PlanSession.ts --duration 120 --spot IAPI --day flat builds this automatically — pulls primaries from active focus, surfaces convert/substrate candidates from TRICKMATRIX, and scales the fatigue-curve time boxes to the time you have. Flags: --day flat|transition, --energy low (bad-day rule), --focus "a,b" to override, --write to save the plan as a dated, checkbox-ready note in Sessions/ (phone-viewable in Obsidian).

Juggling the four disciplines (the cross-session layer)

Everything above is within a session. This is across sessions: how flatground, transition, ledge/rail, and gap share a week without fragmenting progress. The doctrine is WISDOM #14; this is how to run it.

The model in one line: flatground is the daily anchor · the spot picks the primary · high-consequence disciplines only on fresh legs · ≤2–3 frontiers across the whole week.

Why not "one day each" or "one week per discipline": both impose a calendar on an opportunistic activity. They lose the other disciplines while you focus, ignore that progress is non-linear, and kill the stoke that actually keeps me skating — and my real bottleneck is consistency + context-switching, not effort. Replace the calendar with two rules: an anchor and an opportunistic primary.

The frequency tiers (set by consequence — WISDOM #14):

Discipline Consequence Frequency When in the session
Flatground low every session (the anchor) warm-up + substrate block, always
Transition low daily-capable any time; great primary on low-energy days
Ledge medium a couple times a week mid-session, warmed up
Rail / gap high when fresh + feeling it early only, fresh legs — never tired, never the closer

A week that respects this (4–5 sessions — emergent, not scheduled):

  • Every session: flatground anchor — substrate block + one flat frontier (WISDOM #3).
  • 2× flat days (IAPI smooth basalt): flatground primary (switch kickflip); add the ledge frontier on the benches if legs are fresh.
  • 1–2× transition days (ORLA low tranny / IAPI mini ramp): transition primary (BS disaster / FS tailstall) — low consequence, high rep.
  • Occasionally, early + fresh only: one rail/gap fear-ladder rep (WISDOM #4) — a commitment deposit, not a frontier.
  • Log loosely in TRICKMATRIX.md; don't force the schedule.

The cap that matters most for me: choose the 2–3 frontiers for the whole week — ideally one per discipline you're actively pushing, not four flatground tricks at once. The current grounded picks live in ASSESSMENT.md → Discipline frontier map.

Tracking proficiency — x/10 landed, filmed

Retired the old 100× / drop-to-5 / drop-to-1 ladder — it assumed open-ended reps and didn't fit narrow, time-boxed park sessions (max ~2h). The method now matches how I actually skate: time-boxed blocks, film, analyze.

  • Inside a trick's block, take ~10 attempts and log how many you land: x/10 in TRICKMATRIX.md. That ratio is the proficiency.
  • Film the block, review between batches — the mid-session footage review is the highest-leverage thing a 2h session unlocks. Pick the one fixable thing.
  • 8/10 both stances = ✅ owned (WISDOM #2). Then it lives in cool-down lines, not project blocks.
  • Don't chase a fixed attempt count — the time box ends the block. Progress compounds across sessions, not within one.

Session template — "Standard 75-min" (run this today)

Tune the trick names to your ASSESSMENT focus. This is the default shape.

Block Time What Why (WISDOM #)
Warm-up 10 min Roll, push switch, easy ollies + manuals. Get loose, no pressure. consolidation/injury
Substrate block 15 min Pop height (ollies over a stick) + weak-stance reps of an owned trick. Film a few. #3 floor-raising, #1 film
Primary trick 25 min The #1 focus trick. Film every batch, review between. Diagnose down the graph if stuck. Target 8/10 both stances. #1, #2, #5
Fear-ladder rep 10 min One controlled scary thing at the edge of comfort (bigger set / commit the slide / drop-in). Small, deliberate. #4
Cool-down line 10 min Link 2–3 owned tricks into a clean line. End on a make. #2 ownership, #7 end-on-make
Log 5 min Jot what worked / one thing to fix next time. Note any status change for ASSESSMENT. feedback loop

Quick variants

  • Short 30-min: warm-up (5) → substrate (10) → primary trick filmed (12) → end-on-a-make (3). Skip fear rep if rushed.
  • Transition day: swap primary block for drop-in / kickturn / pumping; fear rep is the bigger ramp.
  • Switch/weak-stance day: entire primary block is switch reps of owned tricks — the cheapest vocabulary doubling (WISDOM #3).
  • "Bad day" rule: if fear/fatigue is high, cut to warm-up + cool-down line, end on a make, go home. A degraded session encodes degraded patterns.

Drills (mined from Norman Woods' Rookie Mistakes)

  • Flatground bookend reps (Rogers protocol): land 10 clean ollies + 10 shuvits at the start and again at the end of every session (scale to your ceiling — if kickflip is the ceiling, 10+10 of those; advanced = the "50 kickflips" game a couple times a week). Reset rule: don't grind a trick down stairs rep after rep — it chews your body up. Try it down; if you catch-and-kick-away (don't land), go do 2–3 clean ones on flat, reset, then go back. Only keep repeating down once you're actually landing on the board.
  • Truck-tightness diagnostic (do before blaming yourself — WISDOM #10): ollie in place; if the board rocks side-to-side as you squat, it's too loose. Tighten a half-turn on both trucks, retest, then quarter-turns until it's stable. (Front truck loosens fastest — recheck periodically.)
  • Staged movement-builder (for any new mechanic — WISDOM #5): off-board crouch, mirror-check that your back is straight → on the board on grass/foam → add a tail-press so the nose lifts → concrete → slight roll → full trick. Same range of motion, ported up the difficulty ladder.
  • Warm-up = reps on flat first (WISDOM #11): before any stairs/rail attempt, rep the trick on smooth flat ground. Most injuries come from showing up cold and committing to the real thing.

Drills & blocks (mined from Rodney Mullen — The Mutt)

  • Centering drill (substrate block — WISDOM #15, new [SKILL] Centering): slow 360 shuvits / pivots / tic-tac spins with eyes on the horizon, hunting the spin axis under your feet. Mullen broke a months-long 360 plateau the day he "found his center" — centering pays off across spins, manuals, and clean catches. 2–3 min, every session.
  • Distance-calibration drill (new [SKILL]): roll up to a fixed mark and stop your front trucks exactly on it; then ollie to land on a measured line / small gap. Trains the perceptual skill of judging the space the board needs — separate from the trick. Especially when crossing into a discipline you're new at (WISDOM #17), this is often the real bottleneck, not the trick.
  • New-trick exploration block (last block of the session): reserve the final ~10–15 min, when you're warmest and loosest, for playing — a new trick, a recombination (WISDOM #16), a "what if I…". Mullen's day was warm-up → drill segments to automaticity → final block for new tricks, "the most intense and most enjoyable." Protects the play (WISDOM #19) and points invention at fresh legs.
  • Off-board / mental / fingerboard reps: between sessions, rehearse the motion in your head and flip a fingerboard to map how weight shifts move the board, then transfer the pattern. Mullen "memorized a pattern for later" this way. Free reps that cost no body.
  • Simulate the environment (for a pressure trick): if a trick has to come out at a specific spot/contest, rehearse the exact stance, speed, terrain, even the noise (WISDOM #6 taken to the extreme — Mullen ran routines hundreds of hours in a mirror and recorded the venue's radio to skate to).

Drill library (migrated from Trello)

Flick drills — the kickflip/flip progression (from the YouTube breakdown ETiQ8bSPJZs; run in order, each isolates one piece of the flick):

  1. Tail Stop Flick (2:22) — learn where the shoe sits on the griptape to initiate the flip; feel the leg rotation and the natural arc of your foot.
  2. Manual Ankle Roll (6:02) — do a small manual while rolling your ankle; use knee + foot placement to feel the board's tension and control the flick.
  3. Pump Fake (8:30) — put your foot exactly where you'd flick from, but hit that spot without releasing/flipping. Builds strike-location consistency. (The key drill.)
  4. Slow Flick (12:11) — get the board to roll over slowly and easily in the air, using the tension from the earlier drills instead of brute force.
  5. Super Flick (14:32) — flip with more power to feel how your legs react under intensity — and learn that efficiency beats sheer strength.

Balance drills:

  • Ride fast with feet closer — narrow stance at speed.
  • Ride a mini ramp with feet closer — same, on transition.
  • Crouched manual — manual held low for control.
  • Manual with feet closer — narrow-stance manual balance.
  • Lateral one-leg balance — single-leg stability off the board.

Post-session (feeds the loop)

  • Review footage — pick the one fixable thing for next time.
  • Update ASSESSMENT.md if anything moved (🔧 → ✅, new 🎯).
  • Drop a JOURNAL note if a session taught a generalizable lesson → it may become a WISDOM principle.
  • Notebook + self-grade (Mullen's report card): two lines — what a variable did today (e.g. "looser trucks → more flow but flick slipped") + how long a problem took to fix — then grade the session A–F. Mullen kept a skating notebook and "rated the entire session… as though it was a school report card." The grade isn't judgment; it's a fast, honest signal of whether the structure worked, separate from whether tricks landed.

Plan log

(optional: jot the date + the session plan you ran, so patterns emerge)

  • 2026-06-24 — Goals split by terrain (switch kickflip / FS flip / BS bigspin = flatground; BS disaster = transition). Two shapes:
    • Flatground day: truck check → warm-up → switch bookend reps → BS bigspin close (10m) → switch kickflip primary (22m, filmed) → FS flip focused block (13m) → cool-down line, end on a make.
    • Transition day: warm-up pumping/kickturns → axle-stall + rock-to-fakie reps → BS disaster focused block (commitment, filmed) → end on a clean roll-away.

Block Time What Why (WISDOM #) Warm-up 10 min Roll, push switch, easy ollies + manuals. Get loose, no pressure. consolidation/injury Substrate block 15 min Pop height (ollies over a stick) + weak-stance reps of an owned trick. Film a few. #3 floor-raising, #1 film Primary trick 25 min The #1 focus trick. Film every batch, review between. Diagnose down the graph if stuck. Target 8/10 both stances. #1, #2, #5 Fear-ladder rep 10 min One controlled scary thing at the edge of comfort (bigger set / commit the slide / drop-in). Small, deliberate. #4 Cool-down line 10 min Link 2–3 owned tricks into a clean line. End on a make. #2 ownership, #7 end-on-make Log 5 min Jot what worked / one thing to fix next time. Note any status change for ASSESSMENT. feedback loop Quick variants Short 30-min: warm-up (5) → substrate (10) → primary trick filmed (12) → end-on-a-make (3). Skip fear rep if rushed. Transition day: swap primary block for drop-in / kickturn / pumping; fear rep is the bigger ramp. Switch/weak-stance day: entire primary block is switch reps of owned tricks — the cheapest vocabulary doubling (WISDOM #3). Bad day rule: if fear/fatigue is high, cut to warm-up + cool-down line, end on a make, go home. A degraded session encodes degraded patterns. Drills (mined from Norman Woods' Rookie Mistakes) Flatground bookend reps (Rogers protocol): land 10 clean ollies + 10 shuvits at the start and again at the end of every session (scale to your ceiling — if kickflip is the ceiling, 10+10 of those; advanced = the 50 kickflips game a couple times a week). Reset rule: don't grind a trick down stairs rep after rep — it chews your body up. Try it down; if you catch-and-kick-away (don't land), go do 2–3 clean ones on flat, reset, then go back. Only keep repeating down once you're actually landing on the board. Truck-tightness diagnostic (do before blaming yourself — WISDOM #10): ollie in place; if the board rocks side-to-side as you squat, it's too loose. Tighten a half-turn on both trucks , retest, then quarter-turns until it's stable. (Front truck loosens fastest — recheck periodically.) Staged movement-builder (for any new mechanic — WISDOM #5): off-board crouch, mirror-check that your back is straight → on the board on grass/foam → add a tail-press so the nose lifts → concrete → slight roll → full trick. Same range of motion, ported up the difficulty ladder. Warm-up = reps on flat first (WISDOM #11): before any stairs/rail attempt, rep the trick on smooth flat ground. Most injuries come from showing up cold and committing to the real thing. Drills blocks (mined from Rodney Mullen — The Mutt ) Centering drill (substrate block — WISDOM #15, new [SKILL] Centering): slow 360 shuvits / pivots / tic-tac spins with eyes on the horizon, hunting the spin axis under your feet . Mullen broke a months-long 360 plateau the day he found his center — centering pays off across spins, manuals, and clean catches. 2–3 min, every session. Distance-calibration drill (new [SKILL] ): roll up to a fixed mark and stop your front trucks exactly on it; then ollie to land on a measured line / small gap. Trains the perceptual skill of judging the space the board needs — separate from the trick. Especially when crossing into a discipline you're new at (WISDOM #17), this is often the real bottleneck, not the trick. New-trick exploration block (last block of the session): reserve the final ~10–15 min, when you're warmest and loosest, for playing — a new trick, a recombination ( WISDOM #16 ), a what if I… . Mullen's day was warm-up → drill segments to automaticity → final block for new tricks, the most intense and most enjoyable. Protects the play (WISDOM #19) and points invention at fresh legs. Off-board / mental / fingerboard reps: between sessions, rehearse the motion in your head and flip a fingerboard to map how weight shifts move the board, then transfer the pattern. Mullen memorized a pattern for later this way. Free reps that cost no body. Simulate the environment (for a pressure trick): if a trick has to come out at a specific spot/contest, rehearse the exact stance, speed, terrain, even the noise (WISDOM #6 taken to the extreme — Mullen ran routines hundreds of hours in a mirror and recorded the venue's radio to skate to). Drill library (migrated from Trello) Flick drills — the kickflip/flip progression (from the YouTube breakdown ETiQ8bSPJZs ; run in order, each isolates one piece of the flick): Tail Stop Flick (2:22) — learn where the shoe sits on the griptape to initiate the flip; feel the leg rotation and the natural arc of your foot. Manual Ankle Roll (6:02) — do a small manual while rolling your ankle; use knee + foot placement to feel the board's tension and control the flick. Pump Fake (8:30) — put your foot exactly where you'd flick from, but hit that spot without releasing/flipping. Builds strike-location consistency. (The key drill.) Slow Flick (12:11) — get the board to roll over slowly and easily in the air, using the tension from the earlier drills instead of brute force. Super Flick (14:32) — flip with more power to feel how your legs react under intensity — and learn that efficiency beats sheer strength. Balance drills: Ride fast with feet closer — narrow stance at speed. Ride a mini ramp with feet closer — same, on transition. Crouched manual — manual held low for control. Manual with feet closer — narrow-stance manual balance. Lateral one-leg balance — single-leg stability off the board. Post-session (feeds the loop) Review footage — pick the one fixable thing for next time. Update ASSESSMENT.md if anything moved (🔧 → ✅, new 🎯). Drop a JOURNAL note if a session taught a generalizable lesson → it may become a WISDOM principle. Notebook + self-grade (Mullen's report card): two lines — what a variable did today (e.g. looser trucks → more flow but flick slipped ) + how long a problem took to fix — then grade the session A–F. Mullen kept a skating notebook and rated the entire session… as though it was a school report card. The grade isn't judgment; it's a fast, honest signal of whether the structure worked, separate from whether tricks landed. Plan log (optional: jot the date + the session plan you ran, so patterns emerge) 2026-06-24 — Goals split by terrain (switch kickflip / FS flip / BS bigspin = flatground; BS disaster = transition). Two shapes: Flatground day: truck check → warm-up → switch bookend reps → BS bigspin close (10m) → switch kickflip primary (22m, filmed) → FS flip focused block (13m) → cool-down line, end on a make. Transition day: warm-up pumping/kickturns → axle-stall + rock-to-fakie reps → BS disaster focused block (commitment, filmed) → end on a clean roll-away.

sections.title: Log — 2026-06-25-2h-flatground
sections.html:
Skate Session — 2h Flatground Day

Date: 2026-06-25 · Length: 2h · Terrain: Flatground

The big unlock of a 2h session: it's the first one long enough to fit a mid-session footage review. That review loop is worth more than doubling your reps. Precision tricks go first, while fresh; volume and commitment go after.

Goals served: switch kickflip · frontside flip · backside bigspin (BS disaster = transition day — see the mini-ramp swap at the bottom if your spot has one.)

The plan (fatigue-curve order)

  • Truck check + warm-up — 12 min Ollie-in-place truck-tightness diagnostic (board rocks side-to-side = too loose, tighten half-turn). Then roll, push switch, easy ollies + manuals. Loosen up, no pressure.

  • Switch bookend reps — 13 min 10 clean switch ollies + 10 switch shuvits (or your switch ceiling). Sets the floor, films your baseline.

  • 🎯 Switch kickflip — 28 min (PRIMARY, fresh legs) Film every batch, review between batches. If stuck, drop down the graph — which movement is failing? Isolate it. Target ownership: 8/10.

  • 🎯 Frontside flip — 22 min (fresh legs) Shoulder wind-and-unwind + commitment. Film batches. Stay patient — this is a feel trick.

  • 🎥 Rest + footage review — 8 min Sit, breathe, watch what you just filmed. Pick the one fixable thing for the back half. This is the block that makes the 2h worth it.

  • 🔧 Backside bigspin — 18 min (close) Already almost owned — push it to 8/10. Apply whatever the footage review just told you.

  • Commitment + free skate — 12 min One fear-ladder rep at the edge of comfort, then just skate and have fun. (Mini-ramp swap below.)

  • Cool-down line + bookend close — 7 min Link 2–3 owned tricks into a clean line + close with 10 clean ollies. End on a make.

Total: 120 min

If your spot has a mini-ramp

Swap the Commitment + free skate block for a transition segment — axle stall + rock-to-fakie reps → one backside disaster focused block (commitment, filmed). That hits all 4 goals in one session instead of three.

Results — what actually happened (Tiago's report)

Trick Outcome Read
🎯 Switch kickflip 0 landed — but becoming easier, feeling more natural Feel is arriving before the make. Classic pre-breakthrough — the catch/commitment is wiring in. Don't force it; the makes follow the feel.
🎯 Frontside flip Not landed — but great adjustments, feels like it'll be easier next session Adjustment phase paid off. Came in for the feel-and-commitment, left with a better motor pattern. Next session opens warmer.
🔧 Backside bigspin 5/10 — becoming easier Halfway to ownership (8/10). Consistency is the only gap now, not the trick.
🔧 Backside disaster 5/10 — did a couple Holding ~50% roll-away. Steady, needs more committed reps to climb.

Headline: no makes on the two flip goals, but both are trending right — switch kickflip feels natural and FS flip got real adjustments. The two rotation tricks both sit at a clean 5/10. Nothing flipped to ✅, but everything moved the right direction. This is what a productive non-PR session looks like.

After (feeds the loop)

  • Review footage → N/A — didn't film this session. (The mid-session footage review is the block that makes a 2h day worth the length — worth bringing a phone/tripod next time, especially for switch kickflip where seeing the catch fail is the whole game.)
  • Update ASSESSMENT if anything moved (logged 2026-06-25 snapshot — trends + 5/10 numbers, no status flips)
  • Journal note if the session taught a generalizable lesson (candidate: "feel arrives before the make" — promote to WISDOM only if it recurs)

Source: full method + WISDOM principles live in ~/.claude/PAI/USER/SKATEBOARDING/ on the Mac.

sections.title: Spots
sections.html:
Spots — Where I Skate, and What Each Is Good For

A spot is a tool. The right session at the wrong spot wastes the day. This file tags each spot with what it's good for so SESSIONS.md can pick the spot that matches the day's goal — and so a low-consequence feature (like a mellow transition for stall reps) gets used deliberately, not by accident.

How to read an entry

Each spot lists:

  • Type — street plaza / transition / mini-ramp / ledges / mixed.
  • Features — the obstacles actually there (ledges, rails, banks, transition height, flatground quality).
  • Best for — which TRICKS.md nodes / ASSESSMENT.md goals this spot is the right place to grind, and why.
  • Consequence — how much a bail costs (low = hammer reps fearlessly; high = bring commitment + film).
  • Logistics — surface, crowd, best time, wax, getting there.

Tags (greppable): #flatground #transition #miniramp #ledges #rails #banks #low-consequence #high-rep #stalls #switch — filter the list by what today's session needs.

⚠️ Entries marked (to confirm) are placeholders — I haven't logged the real details yet. Fill them from memory or on the next visit; honesty over a guessed layout.

ORLA — Megapista da Orla (Skate Park da Orla do Guaíba)

Porto Alegre · Trecho 3, Praia de Belas · Av. Edvaldo Pereira Paiva, 960 · Tags: #transition #bowl #ledges #flatground #low-consequence #high-rep #stalls

  • Type: Mixed mega-park — the biggest in Latin America (6,200 m², opened Oct 2021, CBSk-certified, hosts STU National 2026). Open 24h.
  • Features: Five formats — three transition (bowl · snake run · flow park) and two street (plaza · flow). Street section has replica obstacles: the Câmara Municipal handrail, Ipanema boardwalk benches, and the curved bridge from Praça Itália. Lakeside, bleachers, parking, food nearby.
    • Transitions of every height — that's the key property: pick the wall height to match the consequence you want. Drop to the lowest for stall/tailstall rep volume; scale up to taller walls and the bowl as the trick gets owned. A spot you don't outgrow.
  • Obstacles — your Trello inventory (31 photos → 📸 SPOTS-ORLA.md): documented obstacles skew street:
    • Ledges / boxes (caixotes): curva, na 45, alto 45 + cotovelo, dois níveis (alto + pequeno), cotovelo, borda pequena, barreira.
    • Stairs + gaps: escadinha, escada pequena e longa, euro gap, gap + caixote, double set (+ rampa / + corrimão longo), queda + corrimão delta.
    • Handrails / rails: corrimão, hubba (+ escadinha + rampa leve), cano.
    • Hydrants: hidrante, hidrante alto com rampa.
    • Benches: bancos. Misc: kong, delta, corrente, cordão descendo a rampa, moeda.
    • Transition pieces: quarter caixote, rampa delta, rampa + tonel médio, mini quarter. (Your photos document street obstacles — for full transition/bowl, the megapista has more than the cards capture.)
  • Best for:
    • 🎯 FS Tailstall (transition) — the reason it's on the list. The low snake-run/flow transitions = low consequence + unlimited reps, exactly how a stall trick gets owned. Grind volume here.
    • 🔧 BS/FS grind (transition) — mellow coping to dial stall-into-grind without the fear tax.
    • 🔧 Backside disaster — safe place to push the commitment rep past 50%.
    • Pumping & carving, kickturns on tranny — the bowl/snake run feed transition fundamentals.
    • Plaza section also covers ledge work (noseslide, 50-50, switch variants) and smooth flatground — so a full goal-spanning session is possible in one spot.
  • Consequence: Low on the mellow transitions (the whole value — bails are cheap, commit fearlessly); higher in the bowl. Pick the section to match the trick.
  • Logistics: 24h · lakeside (wind off the Guaíba can matter) · bleachers + parking + food · (crowd times / wax — confirm on next visit.)
  • Source: Prefeitura de POA + Destino POA, researched 2026-06-25.

IAPI Skate Park

Porto Alegre · Higienópolis · between Rua Dr. Eduardo Chartier and Av. Cristóvão Colombo · Tags: #ledges #rails #flatground #streetplaza #manualpads #transition #miniramp #quarterpipe

  • Type: Street plaza + transition — Brazil's first skate plaza (built 2001, 4,000 m²), a traditional POA meeting spot, renovated 2023 + new obstacle 2024. Not street-only: it also has ramps (see below).
  • Features:
    • Street: polished basalt floor (modeled on Praça da Matriz) — fast, smooth flatground; replica benches (Praça XV), Câmara Municipal handrails, a fire hydrant, and a painted crosswalk. Ledges + handrails + manual-pad-style ground.
    • Transition: two mini ramps (a smaller + a bigger one) and two quarter pipes (one small/mid, one tall) (Tiago's firsthand report).
  • Obstacles — your Trello inventory (24 photos → 📸 SPOTS-IAPI.md):
    • Transition (confirms the firsthand report): Mini ramp + Mini ramp grande · Quarter + Savana + quarter · two Banks · Pirâmide.
    • Ledges / boxes: Two level box, Box grande, Cofre, Bloco, Blocos pequenos, IAPI-curb, Bolo, Muros.
    • Manual pad (the flat-ledge balance surface). Handrails: Corrimão + Corrimão pequeno, Hubba, Hubba + escada + corrimão. Misc: óculos, Pole, Anhangabaú, Banco's.
  • Best for:
    • 🎯 Switch noseslide + 🎯 Switch 50-50 + ➕ FS crooked — the ledges/benches are the right surface to grind these out.
    • 🔧 Switch kickflip + ➕ Nollie FS 180 — the smooth basalt is ideal flatground volume for the flip goals (and filming them).
    • 🎯 FS tailstall + 🔧 BS/FS grind + 🔧 disaster — the smaller mini ramp + small/mid quarter give a low-consequence rep zone, so you don't have to go to ORLA for stall reps. Step up to the bigger mini / tall quarter when owned.
    • Manual / nose-manual work; 50-50 / noseslide (regular) lines for warm-ups.
  • Consequence: Low–moderate — basalt flat, low ledges, and the small ramps are forgiving; handrails and the tall quarter are the high-consequence end. Pick the obstacle to match the trick.
  • Logistics: Higienópolis (close-in neighborhood spot) · fast basalt (slick when waxed/wet) · (crowd times — confirm on next visit.)

Features — the obstacles actually there (ledges, rails, banks, transition height, flatground quality). sections.title: Sources
sections.html:
Sources — Channels & Podcasts I Mine

I maintain this list. It's the input feed for the digestion loop: pick a source → watch/listen → write a JOURNAL entry → extract wisdom into WISDOM.md.

Each entry has a MINE FOR note so digestion is targeted — I watch looking for something specific, not passively. ★ = leverage for me right now (★★★ = highest learning value, ★ = occasional / context). Notes are grounded in what each channel actually produces (researched 2026-06-24), not guesses.

Format: - **Name** (@handle) — ★priority · MINE FOR: <the specific thing this source is best at>

Technique & Tutorials — the deliberate-practice engines

(highest leverage for actually learning tricks · mostly Lane 2 — visual, voice-memo mined — see MINING.md)

  • Skate IQ (@Skateiq) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: structured, confidence-building step-by-step fundamentals tutorials (founder Mitchie Brusco, X-Games medalist). Best for a clean progression on the basics.
  • Whythetrick (@whythetrick) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: the mechanism layer — 3D animation + biomechanics explaining the invisible forces and body positions behind a trick. Go here when a standard tutorial leaves you confused about why (pairs with WISDOM #5: build the movement).
  • Skatepark Lessons (@SkateparkLessons) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: structured tutorials from a 20+ yr coach (JB) on actually using a skatepark; strong beginner→intermediate progression and transition fundamentals.
  • Tom's Tutorials (@toms_tutorials) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: methodical "how to land X" breakdowns from a veteran instructor (Tom Wong); great for trick-acquisition sequencing — the order to learn things.
  • Skate Hacks (@SKATEHACKS) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: slow-motion mechanics + the creator voicing their thought process mid-trick. Best for the feel and timing of a trick, not just the steps.
  • Skate Sage (@SkateSage) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: detailed slow-motion "HOW TO / Explained" breakdowns, one trick per video (grinds, slides, shuvits, flips, nollie). Tight, focused library — pick the exact trick you're working and watch its full slow-mo explanation.
  • Flow Sk8 Academy (@FLOWSK8ACADEMY) — ★★ · MINE FOR: filmed one-on-one coaching sessions on a single trick — useful for seeing the real cueing/feedback loop of instruction. Small library.
  • TR7 Skate (@TR7Skate) — ★★ · MINE FOR: their beginner "how to start / learn tricks" tutorial series (UK shop/park). Rest is shop/scene/edit content — mine the tutorial playlists only.
  • Tom Asta (@tomasta) — ★★ · MINE FOR: a working pro openly grinding out new tricks rep-by-rep — models the iterate-publicly, film-everything process (WISDOM #1), not just polished clips.
  • Maurio McCoy (@maurcoy) — ★★ · MINE FOR: a pro documenting learning new tricks one video at a time — process modeling, persistence, mindset. Weaker on explicit step-by-step.
  • Norman Woods (@NormanWoods) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: two things — (1) talk-heavy skate wisdom, esp. the "Rookie Mistakes" series = pure coaching insight I can auto-mine (Lane 1); (2) visual step-by-step street-trick breakdowns (Lane 2) from a working coach (21 yrs skating, 8 yrs of 1-on-1 lessons, Collage Skateboards). Per-video lane — see MINING.md.

Off-Board — strength, conditioning, longevity

(the other half of a practice system — WISDOM #3 substrate, injury prevention · Lane 1 — I auto-mine the transcript)

  • The Daily Push (@TheDailyPush) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: skate-specific training, recovery, nutrition, mindset + video-analysis methodology (host Matt Beare, personal trainer). The off-board pillar of sustainable progression.
  • Skateboard Strength (@skateboardstrength9834) — ★★★ · MINE FOR: science-backed mobility, pop/power, and injury-prevention work (ankles, hips) that directly supports on-board progression and longevity. Aimed at skaters who want to keep skating for years.

Mindset, Culture & Motivation

(protect motivation, learn how pros think — low direct tutorial value · Lane 1 — I auto-mine the transcript)

  • The Nine Club (@thenineclub) — ★★ · MINE FOR: long-form pro interviews (Chris Roberts) — how top skaters think about progression, fear, injury, and the long game. Mindset wisdom, not trick tips.
  • Progress Daily (@progressdaily100) — ★ · MINE FOR: progress-mindset framing and consistency/habit inspiration, plus scattered trick tips. Motivation more than method.
  • Liam Dawson (@liam_dawson) — ★ · MINE FOR: a committed amateur's real weekly practice rhythm — how a regular skater structures sessions. Motivation, not how-to.
  • Dan Corrigan (@DanCorrigan) — ★ · MINE FOR: skate culture and entertainment (comedy, Game of S.K.A.T.E.). Low instructional value — watch for fun/culture.

Gear & Equipment

(setup decisions, not skill-building)

  • Andrew Cannon (@AndrewCannon) — ★ · MINE FOR: insider gear/product analysis (Santa Cruz / OJ Wheels, former pro) — deep equipment knowledge to inform setup choices.
  • Dale Decker (@daledecker) — ★ · MINE FOR: shoe/gear reviews + casual trick tips in vlog form — useful for equipment decisions.

Books & Long-form

(full-text — Lane 1, I read the whole thing and mine it in parallel passes)

  • Rodney Mullen — The Mutt (2004) — ★★★ · DIGESTED 2026-06-26 (JOURNAL) · MINE FOR: deliberate-practice methodology, trick invention from primitives, relearning/transfer (beginner's mind), the mental game, and longevity/injury. The single richest training-mindset source the system has touched. → WISDOM #15–19 + sharpenings, TRICKS "Trick Invention & Design" + Centering/Distance-Calibration skills, 5 SESSIONS drills.

How to add a source

  1. Drop it under the right section with a ★ priority and a MINE FOR note (what is this source best at for me?).
  2. If it's a one-off great video rather than a recurring channel, skip this file — just journal it directly in JOURNAL/.
  3. Re-rank ★ during review if my focus shifts (e.g. moving from flat-ground to transition changes which sources have leverage).

Watchlist (specific videos to digest next)

(queue individual videos here; clear them as they become JOURNAL entries)

  • (empty — add links as you find them)

sections.title: Mining Method
sections.html:
Mining — How Content Becomes Wisdom

The procedure behind the digestion loop. SOURCES.md is the input feed; this is how I extract from it; JOURNAL/ is where each extraction is recorded; WISDOM.md is where the keepers land.

Core constraint (be honest about it): I can't watch video. A slow-mo kickflip is invisible to me. So the method is tiered by content type — talk survives transcription, visual technique does not. Mode: Hybrid (chosen 2026-06-24).

Core rule: Mining is curation, not collection. Every candidate insight gets Tiago's gut-check before it's committed to WISDOM. Never bulk-dump extracted text — that buries the few principles that matter under noise.

Lane is per-VIDEO, not per-channel. The lane assignments below are a channel's center of gravity, not a rule. A tutorial channel's slow-mo trick is Lane 2; its talking-head wisdom video is Lane 1. Example: Norman Woods is listed under Lane 2 for trick tips, but his "Rookie Mistakes" series is pure talk-wisdom → Lane 1 (I auto-mine it). Always pick the lane from the actual video.

Lane 1 — Talk-heavy → I mine autonomously

Channels: The Nine Club, The Daily Push, Skateboard Strength, Progress Daily, gear talks (Andrew Cannon, Dale Decker). Why: podcasts and verbal content (exercise protocols, mindset, career lessons) survive transcription intact. Procedure:

  1. Tiago names an episode/video (or I pick from a queue).
  2. I pull the transcript (Apify YouTube actor or yt-dlp captions).
  3. Run it through ExtractWisdom (Fabric extract_wisdom pattern).
  4. I hand back candidate wisdom + any specific drills/protocols — a short list, not a wall.
  5. Tiago confirms what's real/useful.
  6. I commit keepers → WISDOM.md (+ TRICKS.md if a drill/node), and log the JOURNAL entry. Yield: a 90-min Nine Club episode → ~6 lines of wisdom, no re-listen.

Lane 2 — Visual technique → Tiago's eyes, my structure

Channels: Whythetrick, Skate Hacks, Skate IQ, Norman Woods, Tom Asta, Maurio McCoy, Flow Sk8 Academy, TR7 Skate. Why: the insight is visual (a flick angle, a shoulder position). A transcript of "flick it like this" is useless. Procedure:

  1. Tiago watches (or skates to it).
  2. Drops a ~20-second voice memo — e.g. "Whythetrick: kickflip flick goes out not up, shoulders stay level." (/audio or the meeting-transcription setup makes this near-zero effort.)
  3. I turn it into a structured JOURNAL entry → WISDOM.md principle → TRICKS.md map update (new movement cue, drill, or edge). Why this fits: matches real life — watch + skate, don't sit and type.

Lane 3 — Talky tutorial → transcript lead, visual confirm

Channels: Tom's Tutorials, Skatepark Lessons. Procedure: I pull the transcript to draft the steps, but flag anything that needs Tiago's eyes to confirm before it becomes wisdom. Hybrid of Lanes 1 and 2.

What goes where (when a candidate insight survives the gut-check)

Insight type Destination
A principle about how to train / what has leverage WISDOM.md (leverage-ranked)
A new trick/movement/drill or a relationship edge TRICKS.md
A drill to slot into a session SESSIONS.md substrate menu
A realization about my current level ASSESSMENT.md
The record of the digestion itself a JOURNAL entry (always)

Starting a mining run

  • Lane 1/3: "Mine this: <url>" — I pull transcript → ExtractWisdom → candidates for your confirm.
  • Lane 2: send the voice memo — I structure it.
  • Queue: drop URLs in the SOURCES.md "Watchlist" and I'll work the queue.

sections.title: Journal — About
sections.html:
Journal — Digesting Content into Wisdom

This is the engine that makes the system grow little by little. Every video or podcast from SOURCES.md becomes one dated entry here, and every entry pushes something into WISDOM.md, TRICKS.md, or ASSESSMENT.md.

The rule

No passive watching. If a piece of content is worth my time, it's worth a 5-minute entry that extracts what it taught and pushes it where it belongs. An undigested video is a coin flip on whether I remember it.

Entry format

Create one file per entry: YYYY-MM-DD-short-slug.md (e.g. 2026-06-25-chris-chann-kickflip.md).

---
date: 2026-06-25
source: Chris Chann — "Kickflip slow-mo breakdown"
link: <url>
type: technique | mindset | drill | progression
---

# <title>

## What it covered
- 2–4 bullets, the substance.

## Wisdom extracted
- The generalizable lesson(s). What changes how I train?
- → **Pushed to WISDOM.md?** [yes — principle #N / sharpened #N / no, too specific]

## Map updates
- New node / edge for TRICKS.md? [e.g. "added DRILL: catch-only flips"] or "none"

## Assessment / session impact
- Does this change my active focus or add a drill to SESSIONS.md? [what]

## One thing to try next session
- The single concrete action.

Why this shape

  • What it covered keeps me honest about substance vs. entertainment.
  • Wisdom extracted + pushed? forces the content to leave the journal and enter the brain of the system (WISDOM) — otherwise notes rot.
  • Map / assessment / session impact closes the loop to action, not just knowledge.
  • One thing to try guarantees every digestion produces a rep.

Index of entries

(newest first — add a line per entry)

  • 2026-06-26 — Rodney Mullen The Mutt (full book, 276pp) → WISDOM #15–19 + sharpenings (#2/#9/#10/#11), TRICKS "Trick Invention & Design" + Centering/Distance-Calibration skills, 5 SESSIONS drills. First full-book run.
  • 2026-06-24 — Norman Woods "Rookie Mistakes" #1–20 (full series) → WISDOM #10–13, Pop-node enrichment + Shoulder Rotation node, 3 SESSIONS drills. First Lane-1 run.

sections.title: The Mutt
sections.html:
The Mutt — Rodney Mullen (full-book digest)

First book mining run. The whole 276-page autobiography was extracted to text and read in 6 parallel passes, each briefed with the existing 14 WISDOM principles so it surfaced only material that confirms / sharpens / adds. Mullen is the highest-value source the system has touched yet: he is, literally, the inventor of the flatground ollie, the kickflip, the 360 flip, the impossible, and the patron saint of obsessive deliberate practice. He treated skating as an engineering problem with a notebook and a report card — which is exactly the posture this whole system is built on.

What it covered (the parts that matter to training)

  • How he invented tricks — by dissecting the board's physics and recombining moves he already owned, not by copying skaters.
  • His practice architecture — timed blocks, run-segments drilled to automaticity, a skating notebook, self-grading.
  • The relearning saga — best freestyler alive, then freestyle died and he rebuilt street skating from zero in his mid-20s. A masterclass on transfer and beginner's mind.
  • The mental game — solitary concentration as a trained skill, the trap of playing it safe, protecting the fun, fear vs. commitment.
  • The body — how he wrecked himself (overtraining, anorexia, a lazy-rep fibula break) and what longevity actually requires.

Wisdom extracted → where it went

→ WISDOM.md (5 new principles + sharpenings)

  • #15 — A plateau is a missing principle, not a rep deficit. "I remember not being able to do more than five and a half 360s after months of trying… Then one day I nearly doubled my record… I had wasted months until I finally stumbled across the 'secret' of simply finding my center." When stuck after honest reps, stop adding volume and hunt the one missing sub-skill.
  • #16 — Invent by recombination; prefer generative primitives. The flatground ollie was vert's tail-scoop ported to flat plus one diagnosed fix: "I just backed my front foot toward the tail, hit the tail harder, and dragged my front foot up the board… The seesaw motion was the essence of the trick." The kickflip came from a bail: "The idea for the ollie kickflip originated from watching how my board flipped and spun when I bailed out of an ollie at its peak." And he chose tricks by generativity: "the kickflip allowed me to expand, the impossible was a more awkward trick." (Mechanics live in TRICKS.)
  • #17 — Mastery doesn't transfer; vocabulary does. "I couldn't skate fast. I couldn't skate on anything higher than a curb… For the first year of trying to street skate, I'd run into whatever I was skating. I looked like I had no depth perception." Yet his freestyle kickflips literally became tech-street's foundation. Cross to a new discipline as a true beginner — but your owned flatground primitives are the substrate that makes the climb fast.
  • #18 — Playing it safe is its own failure mode. "I played it safe and felt like a fraud… I'd go out and do the safest run possible… it sucked all the fun and satisfaction out." His level jumped to Skater of the Year only once he skated for the trick he wanted, not the score. The performance-side complement to the fear ladder (#4).
  • #19 — Protect the play, or the engine seizes. The skaters who lasted "treated skating like a toy." Mullen's relentless outcome-intensity gave him panic attacks and burnout. Guard the fun — it's the fuel under volume (#9), and skating is your most consistently-alive creative spark.
  • Sharpenings folded into existing principles:
    • #2 (ownership): overlearn past the landing — he ran routines eyes-closed; the real marker is automaticity + "can I improvise off it?", and invent-to-own is a years-long gap (invented the impossible in 1982, contest-ready 1986).
    • #9 (volume): "I skated for four hours a day, but in an empty way… like some robot that can't stop." Empty reps build nothing — presence is the active ingredient, and skills rot week-over-week without maintenance.
    • #11 (body/athlete): the worst break of his career came from a lazy, half-committed 360 flip with no slam — "I did it lazily and landed with my back foot off… my fibula was snapped." Lazy reps injure; commit fully even to trivial ones. And intensity without recovery (mile PRs, 3h sleep, anorexia) nearly killed him.
    • #10 (equipment): migrate setup millimeter-by-millimeter over weeks, not in one jump — Ternasky widened his board 1mm/week until he was on a normal street deck.

→ TRICKS.md (new "Trick Invention & Design" section + 2 skill nodes)

  • New reference section on how new tricks are made: port an owned move to a new plane + diagnose the one missing fix; reverse-engineer from your own bails and from still photos; one mechanical reframe often unlocks a trick ("I had to use my rear foot as an axle so that my skateboard continued on a clean rotation as it wrapped around my foot" — the impossible); chase generative primitives over dead-end novelties.
  • New [SKILL] Centering (the master balance fundamental) and [SKILL] Distance / Landing-Zone Calibration (a separate trainable perceptual skill from the trick itself).

→ SESSIONS.md (5 additions)

  • New-trick exploration block — reserve the last slice of a session for inventing/playing, when warm and loose (Mullen's day: warm-up → drill segments to automaticity → final block for new tricks, "the most intense and most enjoyable").
  • Skating notebook + self-grade — "I began keeping a skating notebook… how different foot placement affected tricks, or how long it took me to fix problems… Then I'd rate the entire session, giving myself a grade as though it was a school report card." (This is basically the JOURNAL loop, per-session.)
  • Centering drill in the substrate block.
  • Distance-calibration drill (roll-up-and-stop on a mark, measured gaps).
  • Off-board / mental / fingerboard reps — rehearse the motion in your head and on a fingerboard to map weight shifts before the real board.
  • Simulate the performance environment for a trick you'll need under pressure (extreme specificity — Mullen ran his routine hundreds of hours in a mirror and recorded the venue's radio station to skate to).

Skipped (honesty)

  • Train-without-vision then mirror-relearn (Barry's drill) — fascinating but advanced/coach-supervised; noted in TRICKS' Centering context, not promoted as its own drill.
  • "Stay calm under a feared stimulus drains its power" — real, but it came from a physical-confrontation story; the transferable core is already inside #4 (fear ladder) and #18, so I didn't add a separate principle.
  • External fixed time-blocks beat willpower (college forced inflexible blocks → more consistent) — genuinely relevant to your context-switching, but it's a life-OS scheduling insight, not a skate-training one. Flagged here; belongs in GTD/PlanMyDay if anywhere, not WISDOM.
  • Most biography (Powell-Peralta politics, World Industries, the Almost/Globe business, romance) — off-domain for a training system.

One thing to try next session

Run the Centering drill in your substrate block (slow 360 shuvits / pivots, eyes on the horizon, find the spin axis under your feet), then in your last block do one new-trick exploration rep while warm — and write a two-line notebook grade for the session in the spirit of Mullen's report card.

sections.title: Norman Woods
sections.html:
Norman Woods — Rookie Mistakes #1–20 (full series digest)

First Lane-1 mining run. Pulled auto-captions for all 20 videos (~59k words), extracted in parallel, curated to keepers, Tiago approved "all as recommended."

What it covered

The 20 "Rookie Mistakes," by topic: 1 squat lower · 2 rotate shoulders · 3 don't push the board down early · 4 look at your feet · 5 straighten your back (hinge from knees) · 6 get up and skate · 7 use your arms · 8 trucks too loose · 9 take care of your body · 10 popping too high · 11 keep heels up · 12 popping too late · 13 stop rushing past tricks · 14 don't skate with ego · 15 can't land it? try differently · 16 ignore the noise · 17 don't sleep on flatground · 18 keep toes inside · 19 (off-domain: body-as-excuse + filming etiquette) · 20 land on toes/balls of feet.

The headline finding

11 of 20 are about the pop/ollie mechanic. A coach with 8 years of 1-on-1 lessons is telling us the ollie is where rookies lose the most progress. → most of this enriched the TRICKS Pop node, not WISDOM.

Wisdom extracted → where it went

  • WISDOM.md — 4 new principles: #10 remove equipment variables (tighten trucks before blaming technique) · #11 train like an athlete (warm up on flat, stretch hip flexors, cross-train) · #12 ego/risk gate (capacity ≠ opportunity; refuse bad-EV spots) · #13 adaptation loop (vary one variable between attempts; copy your own body). Plus a skate:sit-ratio adherence note folded into #9.
  • TRICKS.md — Pop node enriched with 7 cues (squat deep · heel up · toes centered · pop-as-you-rise timing · only as high as needed · arms drive it · don't push down/absorb). New Shoulder Rotation (Wind-and-Unwind) node. Catch node + landing-on-toes & knee-tuck cues. Level Shoulders + back-under-45° / look-at-feet.
  • SESSIONS.md — 3 drills: flatground bookend reps + Rogers reset · truck-tightness diagnostic · staged movement-builder (+ warm-up-on-flat).

Skipped (honesty)

  • #06, #16 → reinforced existing #9 (volume); only the skate:sit tactic kept.
  • #19 → mostly off-domain (mindset rehash + Instagram filming etiquette); not committed.

One thing to try next session

Run the truck-tightness diagnostic first (might be sabotaging your pop), then do the flatground bookend reps — 10 ollies + 10 shuvits to open, focusing on heel-up + toes-centered + squat-deep, and again to close.
